revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Saidai-ji | native_name = 西大寺 | image = Saidai-ji Nara Japan08bs3.jpg | image_size = 300 | alt = | caption = [[Hondō|Main Hall]] | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Saidai-ji | location = 1-1-5 Saidaiji Shibachō, Nara-shi, Nara-ken | coordinates = {{coord|34|41|37|N|135|46|46.2|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = [[Buddhist]] | rite = [[Shingon Risshu|Shingon Ritsu]] | deity = [[Shaka Nyorai]] (Śākyamuni)
([[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple | website = {{url|http://saidaiji.or.jp/}} | founded_by = [[Jōtō (monk)|Jōtō]] and [[Empress Kōken]] |established=765| year_completed = 1808 (Reconstruction) | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|41|37|N|135|46|46.2|E}}}}} }} [[file:200730 Model of Saidaiji in Heijokyo viewed from north.jpg|thumb|right|280px|Model of Nara period Saidai-ji]] {{nihongo|'''Saidai-ji'''|西大寺}} is a [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] located in the Saidiaji-Shiba neighborhood of the city of [[Nara, Nara|Nara]], [[Nara Prefecture]], Japan. It became the head temple of the {{nihongo|[[Shingon Risshu]]|真言律宗}} sect after the sect's founder, {{nihongo|[[Eison]]|叡尊}}, took over administration in 1238.{{cite book |last=Watt | first=Paul B. |editor-first=George| editor-last=Tanabe |title=Religions of Japan in Practice |publisher=Princeton University Press |date=March 8, 1999 |chapter=Chapter 7: Eison and the Shingon Vinaya Sect |isbn=0-691-05789-3}} The ''[[honzon]]'' of the temple is a statue of [[Shakyamuni|Shaka Nyorai]], enshrined by Eison in 1249. The temple was once one of the powerful [[Nanto Shichi Daiji|Seven Great Temples]] of the ancient capital of [[Heijō-kyō]]. ==History== According to the {{nihongo|''Saidaiji Zaizairyukicho''|西大寺資財流記帳|}} from 780, in September 764 when the retired [[Empress Koken]] requested the construction of a gilt bronze statue of the [[Four Heavenly Kings]] to pray for the end of the [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]]. In October of the same year, Empress Koken re-enthroned as [[Empress Shōtoku]]. The following year, in 765, the aforementioned statues of the Four Heavenly Kings were completed and Saidai-ji Temple was founded. These four statues are still enshrined in Saidai-ji's Shiōdō Hall, but only the demons that each statue steps on are from the time of the temple's founding, and the statues themselves have been replaced by later works. At the time of Saidai-ji's founding, the monk [[Dōkyō]] held great influence in the political world, and it is believed that Dōkyō's ideological influence was also great in the construction of Saidai-ji Temple. The temple's name of "Saidai-ji" is a counterpoint to the great national temple of [[Tōdai-ji]] and thus a challenge to the existing political and religious power structure. The temple in its initial form was a huge complex and was counted as one of the [[Nanto Shichi Daiji|Seven Great Temples of Nara]]. It has two Main Halls: the Yakushi Kondō and the Miroku Kondō, numerous smaller halls (including the Shio-do, Juichimen-do), and twin five-story pagodas on the east and west. According to the "Zaizairyuki-cho", these halls housed many Buddhist statues and were decorated with many mirrors. In the Miroku Kondō alone, a total of 77 Buddhist statues were placed, and the Yakushi Kondō housed 21 statues. However, the temple fell quickly into decline during the [[Heian period]], and many of its halls and pagodas were lost to fires and typhoons, and it came under the control of [[Kofuku-ji]]. The temple was restored in the [[Kamakura period]] by the monk [[Eison]]. He began the restoration in 1238 over a 50 years period worked to transform the temple into a center for social welfare work, especially to help the poor and sick. Many of the Buddhist statues and crafts that remain at the temple today were created during the time of Eison. The temple also produced many noteworthy priests, such as [[Ninsho]], who worked to restore ruined temples of various provinces, including several of the ''[[kokubunji]]'' temples. In the [[Muromachi period]], the temple was burned down in December 1499 during the wars of the early [[Sengoku period]], and its surviving East Pagoda was destroyed by a fire in 1502. During the [[Edo period]], the temple received a fief of 300 ''[[koku]]'' from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and began reconstruction. All of the current temple buildings were rebuilt after the Edo Period. Saidai-ji became independent from the [[Shingon]] sect in June 1895, and established the Shingon Ritsu sect. The temple houses numerous [[National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasures]] and [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]]. The precincts of Saidai-ji were designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1965. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2006|title=西大寺境内|trans-title= Saidai-ji keinai|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2022}} == Building list == *[[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] (Hondō – 本堂) – [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]. It was rebuilt in 1808. *Shiō-dō (四王堂) – It was rebuilt in 1674. *Aizen-dō (愛染堂) – It was reconstructed in 1762. File:Saidai-ji Nara Japan03n.jpg|Shiō-dō File:Saidai-ji5.JPG|Aizen-dō File:Kishi Monju pentad (Saidaiji Nara).jpg|[[Manjushri|Manjushri Bodhisattva]] (Monju Bosatsu) ==See also== * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts-others)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] * [[Nanto Shichi Daiji]], Seven Great Temples of Nanto. * [[Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato]] *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * {{cite web|title=Saidai-ji Temple Homepage| language=Japanese| url=http://www.naranet.co.jp/saidaiji/| accessdate = 2010-02-05}} {{Commons category-inline|Saidaiji (Nara)|Saidai-ji}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Saidai-Ji}} [[Category:8th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Nara, Nara]] [[Category:Shingon Ritsu temples]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:8th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Nara period]] [[Category:765 establishments]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in the 760s]] [[Category:Temples of Gautama Buddha]]

File:Saidai-ji Nara Japan03n.jpg|Shiō-dō File:Saidai-ji5.JPG|Aizen-dō File:Kishi Monju pentad (Saidaiji Nara).jpg|[[Manjushri|Manjushri Bodhisattva]] (Monju Bosatsu) ==See also== * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts-others)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] * [[Nanto Shichi Daiji]], Seven Great Temples of Nanto. * [[Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato]] *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * {{cite web|title=Saidai-ji Temple Homepage| language=Japanese| url=http://www.naranet.co.jp/saidaiji/| accessdate = 2010-02-05}} {{Commons category-inline|Saidaiji (Nara)|Saidai-ji}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Saidai-Ji}} [[Category:8th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Nara, Nara]] [[Category:Shingon Ritsu temples]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:8th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Nara period]] [[Category:765 establishments]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in the 760s]] [[Category:Temples of Gautama Buddha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:pages from wikipedia]][[en:Saidai-ji]][[Category:pages from wikipedia]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Saidai-ji | native_name = 西大寺 | image = Saidai-ji Nara Japan08bs3.jpg | image_size = 300 | alt = | caption = [[Hondō|Main Hall]] | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Saidai-ji | location = 1-1-5 Saidaiji Shibachō, Nara-shi, Nara-ken | coordinates = {{coord|34|41|37|N|135|46|46.2|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = [[Buddhist]] | rite = [[Shingon Risshu|Shingon Ritsu]] | deity = [[Shaka Nyorai]] (Śākyamuni)
([[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple | website = {{url|http://saidaiji.or.jp/}} | founded_by = [[Jōtō (monk)|Jōtō]] and [[Empress Kōken]] |established=765| year_completed = 1808 (Reconstruction) | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|41|37|N|135|46|46.2|E}}}}} }} [[file:200730 Model of Saidaiji in Heijokyo viewed from north.jpg|thumb|right|280px|Model of Nara period Saidai-ji]] {{nihongo|'''Saidai-ji'''|西大寺}} is a [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] located in the Saidiaji-Shiba neighborhood of the city of [[Nara, Nara|Nara]], [[Nara Prefecture]], Japan. It became the head temple of the {{nihongo|[[Shingon Risshu]]|真言律宗}} sect after the sect's founder, {{nihongo|[[Eison]]|叡尊}}, took over administration in 1238.{{cite book |last=Watt | first=Paul B. |editor-first=George| editor-last=Tanabe |title=Religions of Japan in Practice |publisher=Princeton University Press |date=March 8, 1999 |chapter=Chapter 7: Eison and the Shingon Vinaya Sect |isbn=0-691-05789-3}} The ''[[honzon]]'' of the temple is a statue of [[Shakyamuni|Shaka Nyorai]], enshrined by Eison in 1249. The temple was once one of the powerful [[Nanto Shichi Daiji|Seven Great Temples]] of the ancient capital of [[Heijō-kyō]]. ==History== According to the {{nihongo|''Saidaiji Zaizairyukicho''|西大寺資財流記帳|}} from 780, in September 764 when the retired [[Empress Koken]] requested the construction of a gilt bronze statue of the [[Four Heavenly Kings]] to pray for the end of the [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]]. In October of the same year, Empress Koken re-enthroned as [[Empress Shōtoku]]. The following year, in 765, the aforementioned statues of the Four Heavenly Kings were completed and Saidai-ji Temple was founded. These four statues are still enshrined in Saidai-ji's Shiōdō Hall, but only the demons that each statue steps on are from the time of the temple's founding, and the statues themselves have been replaced by later works. At the time of Saidai-ji's founding, the monk [[Dōkyō]] held great influence in the political world, and it is believed that Dōkyō's ideological influence was also great in the construction of Saidai-ji Temple. The temple's name of "Saidai-ji" is a counterpoint to the great national temple of [[Tōdai-ji]] and thus a challenge to the existing political and religious power structure. The temple in its initial form was a huge complex and was counted as one of the [[Nanto Shichi Daiji|Seven Great Temples of Nara]]. It has two Main Halls: the Yakushi Kondō and the Miroku Kondō, numerous smaller halls (including the Shio-do, Juichimen-do), and twin five-story pagodas on the east and west. According to the "Zaizairyuki-cho", these halls housed many Buddhist statues and were decorated with many mirrors. In the Miroku Kondō alone, a total of 77 Buddhist statues were placed, and the Yakushi Kondō housed 21 statues. However, the temple fell quickly into decline during the [[Heian period]], and many of its halls and pagodas were lost to fires and typhoons, and it came under the control of [[Kofuku-ji]]. The temple was restored in the [[Kamakura period]] by the monk [[Eison]]. He began the restoration in 1238 over a 50 years period worked to transform the temple into a center for social welfare work, especially to help the poor and sick. Many of the Buddhist statues and crafts that remain at the temple today were created during the time of Eison. The temple also produced many noteworthy priests, such as [[Ninsho]], who worked to restore ruined temples of various provinces, including several of the ''[[kokubunji]]'' temples. In the [[Muromachi period]], the temple was burned down in December 1499 during the wars of the early [[Sengoku period]], and its surviving East Pagoda was destroyed by a fire in 1502. During the [[Edo period]], the temple received a fief of 300 ''[[koku]]'' from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and began reconstruction. All of the current temple buildings were rebuilt after the Edo Period. Saidai-ji became independent from the [[Shingon]] sect in June 1895, and established the Shingon Ritsu sect. The temple houses numerous [[National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasures]] and [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]]. The precincts of Saidai-ji were designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1965. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2006|title=西大寺境内|trans-title= Saidai-ji keinai|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2022}} == Building list == *[[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] (Hondō – 本堂) – [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]. It was rebuilt in 1808. *Shiō-dō (四王堂) – It was rebuilt in 1674. *Aizen-dō (愛染堂) – It was reconstructed in 1762. File:Saidai-ji Nara Japan03n.jpg|Shiō-dō File:Saidai-ji5.JPG|Aizen-dō File:Kishi Monju pentad (Saidaiji Nara).jpg|[[Manjushri|Manjushri Bodhisattva]] (Monju Bosatsu) ==See also== * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts-others)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] * [[Nanto Shichi Daiji]], Seven Great Temples of Nanto. * [[Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato]] *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * {{cite web|title=Saidai-ji Temple Homepage| language=Japanese| url=http://www.naranet.co.jp/saidaiji/| accessdate = 2010-02-05}} {{Commons category-inline|Saidaiji (Nara)|Saidai-ji}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Saidai-Ji}} [[Category:8th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Nara, Nara]] [[Category:Shingon Ritsu temples]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:8th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Nara period]] [[Category:765 establishments]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in the 760s]] [[Category:Temples of Gautama Buddha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia22]] [[en:Saidai-ji]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia22]]

At the time of Saidai-ji's founding, the monk [[Dōkyō]] held great influence in the political world, and it is believed that Dōkyō's ideological influence was also great in the construction of Saidai-ji Temple. The temple's name of "Saidai-ji" is a counterpoint to the great national temple of [[Tōdai-ji]] and thus a challenge to the existing political and religious power structure. The temple in its initial form was a huge complex and was counted as one of the [[Nanto Shichi Daiji|Seven Great Temples of Nara]]. It has two Main Halls: the Yakushi Kondō and the Miroku Kondō, numerous smaller halls (including the Shio-do, Juichimen-do), and twin five-story pagodas on the east and west. According to the "Zaizairyuki-cho", these halls housed many Buddhist statues and were decorated with many mirrors. In the Miroku Kondō alone, a total of 77 Buddhist statues were placed, and the Yakushi Kondō housed 21 statues. However, the temple fell quickly into decline during the [[Heian period]], and many of its halls and pagodas were lost to fires and typhoons, and it came under the control of [[Kofuku-ji]]. The temple was restored in the [[Kamakura period]] by the monk [[Eison]]. He began the restoration in 1238 over a 50 years period worked to transform the temple into a center for social welfare work, especially to help the poor and sick. Many of the Buddhist statues and crafts that remain at the temple today were created during the time of Eison. The temple also produced many noteworthy priests, such as [[Ninsho]], who worked to restore ruined temples of various provinces, including several of the ''[[kokubunji]]'' temples. In the [[Muromachi period]], the temple was burned down in December 1499 during the wars of the early [[Sengoku period]], and its surviving East Pagoda was destroyed by a fire in 1502. During the [[Edo period]], the temple received a fief of 300 ''[[koku]]'' from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and began reconstruction. All of the current temple buildings were rebuilt after the Edo Period. Saidai-ji became independent from the [[Shingon]] sect in June 1895, and established the Shingon Ritsu sect. The temple houses numerous [[National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasures]] and [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]]. The precincts of Saidai-ji were designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1965. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2006|title=西大寺境内|trans-title= Saidai-ji keinai|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2022}} == Building list == *[[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] (Hondō – 本堂) – [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]. It was rebuilt in 1808. *Shiō-dō (四王堂) – It was rebuilt in 1674. *Aizen-dō (愛染堂) – It was reconstructed in 1762. File:Saidai-ji Nara Japan03n.jpg|Shiō-dō File:Saidai-ji5.JPG|Aizen-dō File:Kishi Monju pentad (Saidaiji Nara).jpg|[[Manjushri|Manjushri Bodhisattva]] (Monju Bosatsu) ==See also== * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts-others)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] * [[Nanto Shichi Daiji]], Seven Great Temples of Nanto. * [[Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato]] *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * {{cite web|title=Saidai-ji Temple Homepage| language=Japanese| url=http://www.naranet.co.jp/saidaiji/| accessdate = 2010-02-05}} {{Commons category-inline|Saidaiji (Nara)|Saidai-ji}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Saidai-Ji}} [[Category:8th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Nara, Nara]] [[Category:Shingon Ritsu temples]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:8th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Nara period]] [[Category:765 establishments]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in the 760s]] [[Category:Temples of Gautama Buddha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia40]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia22]] [[en:Saidai-ji]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia22]] [[Category:dd]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Saidai-ji | native_name = 西大寺 | image = Saidai-ji Nara Japan08bs3.jpg | image_size = 300 | alt = | caption = [[Hondō|Main Hall]] | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Saidai-ji | location = 1-1-5 Saidaiji Shibachō, Nara-shi, Nara-ken | coordinates = {{coord|34|41|37|N|135|46|46.2|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = [[Buddhist]] | rite = [[Shingon Risshu|Shingon Ritsu]] | deity = [[Shaka Nyorai]] (Śākyamuni)
([[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple | website = {{url|http://saidaiji.or.jp/}} | founded_by = [[Jōtō (monk)|Jōtō]] and [[Empress Kōken]] |established=765| year_completed = 1808 (Reconstruction) | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|41|37|N|135|46|46.2|E}}}}} }} [[file:200730 Model of Saidaiji in Heijokyo viewed from north.jpg|thumb|right|280px|Model of Nara period Saidai-ji]] {{nihongo|'''Saidai-ji'''|西大寺}} is a [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] located in the Saidiaji-Shiba neighborhood of the city of [[Nara, Nara|Nara]], [[Nara Prefecture]], Japan. It became the head temple of the {{nihongo|[[Shingon Risshu]]|真言律宗}} sect after the sect's founder, {{nihongo|[[Eison]]|叡尊}}, took over administration in 1238.{{cite book |last=Watt | first=Paul B. |editor-first=George| editor-last=Tanabe |title=Religions of Japan in Practice |publisher=Princeton University Press |date=March 8, 1999 |chapter=Chapter 7: Eison and the Shingon Vinaya Sect |isbn=0-691-05789-3}} The ''[[honzon]]'' of the temple is a statue of [[Shakyamuni|Shaka Nyorai]], enshrined by Eison in 1249. The temple was once one of the powerful [[Nanto Shichi Daiji|Seven Great Temples]] of the ancient capital of [[Heijō-kyō]]. ==History== According to the {{nihongo|''Saidaiji Zaizairyukicho''|西大寺資財流記帳|}} from 780, in September 764 when the retired [[Empress Koken]] requested the construction of a gilt bronze statue of the [[Four Heavenly Kings]] to pray for the end of the [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]]. In October of the same year, Empress Koken re-enthroned as [[Empress Shōtoku]]. The following year, in 765, the aforementioned statues of the Four Heavenly Kings were completed and Saidai-ji Temple was founded. These four statues are still enshrined in Saidai-ji's Shiōdō Hall, but only the demons that each statue steps on are from the time of the temple's founding, and the statues themselves have been replaced by later works. At the time of Saidai-ji's founding, the monk [[Dōkyō]] held great influence in the political world, and it is believed that Dōkyō's ideological influence was also great in the construction of Saidai-ji Temple. The temple's name of "Saidai-ji" is a counterpoint to the great national temple of [[Tōdai-ji]] and thus a challenge to the existing political and religious power structure. The temple in its initial form was a huge complex and was counted as one of the [[Nanto Shichi Daiji|Seven Great Temples of Nara]]. It has two Main Halls: the Yakushi Kondō and the Miroku Kondō, numerous smaller halls (including the Shio-do, Juichimen-do), and twin five-story pagodas on the east and west. According to the "Zaizairyuki-cho", these halls housed many Buddhist statues and were decorated with many mirrors. In the Miroku Kondō alone, a total of 77 Buddhist statues were placed, and the Yakushi Kondō housed 21 statues. However, the temple fell quickly into decline during the [[Heian period]], and many of its halls and pagodas were lost to fires and typhoons, and it came under the control of [[Kofuku-ji]]. The temple was restored in the [[Kamakura period]] by the monk [[Eison]]. He began the restoration in 1238 over a 50 years period worked to transform the temple into a center for social welfare work, especially to help the poor and sick. Many of the Buddhist statues and crafts that remain at the temple today were created during the time of Eison. The temple also produced many noteworthy priests, such as [[Ninsho]], who worked to restore ruined temples of various provinces, including several of the ''[[kokubunji]]'' temples. In the [[Muromachi period]], the temple was burned down in December 1499 during the wars of the early [[Sengoku period]], and its surviving East Pagoda was destroyed by a fire in 1502. During the [[Edo period]], the temple received a fief of 300 ''[[koku]]'' from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and began reconstruction. All of the current temple buildings were rebuilt after the Edo Period. Saidai-ji became independent from the [[Shingon]] sect in June 1895, and established the Shingon Ritsu sect. The temple houses numerous [[National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasures]] and [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]]. The precincts of Saidai-ji were designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1965. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2006|title=西大寺境内|trans-title= Saidai-ji keinai|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2022}} == Building list == *[[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] (Hondō – 本堂) – [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]. It was rebuilt in 1808. *Shiō-dō (四王堂) – It was rebuilt in 1674. *Aizen-dō (愛染堂) – It was reconstructed in 1762. File:Saidai-ji Nara Japan03n.jpg|Shiō-dō File:Saidai-ji5.JPG|Aizen-dō File:Kishi Monju pentad (Saidaiji Nara).jpg|[[Manjushri|Manjushri Bodhisattva]] (Monju Bosatsu) ==See also== * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts-others)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] * [[Nanto Shichi Daiji]], Seven Great Temples of Nanto. * [[Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato]] *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * {{cite web|title=Saidai-ji Temple Homepage| language=Japanese| url=http://www.naranet.co.jp/saidaiji/| accessdate = 2010-02-05}} {{Commons category-inline|Saidaiji (Nara)|Saidai-ji}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Saidai-Ji}} [[Category:8th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Nara, Nara]] [[Category:Shingon Ritsu temples]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:8th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Nara period]] [[Category:765 establishments]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in the 760s]] [[Category:Temples of Gautama Buddha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia40]] [[Category:Completed pages from wikipedia]] [[en:Saidai-ji]] [[Category:dd]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Saidai-ji | native_name = 西大寺 | image = Saidai-ji Nara Japan08bs3.jpg | image_size = 300 | alt = | caption = [[Hondō|Main Hall]] | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Saidai-ji | location = 1-1-5 Saidaiji Shibachō, Nara-shi, Nara-ken | coordinates = {{coord|34|41|37|N|135|46|46.2|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = [[Buddhist]] | rite = [[Shingon Risshu|Shingon Ritsu]] | deity = [[Shaka Nyorai]] (Śākyamuni)
([[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple | website = {{url|http://saidaiji.or.jp/}} | founded_by = [[Jōtō (monk)|Jōtō]] and [[Empress Kōken]] |established=765| year_completed = 1808 (Reconstruction) | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|41|37|N|135|46|46.2|E}}}}} }} [[file:200730 Model of Saidaiji in Heijokyo viewed from north.jpg|thumb|right|280px|Model of Nara period Saidai-ji]] {{nihongo|'''Saidai-ji'''|西大寺}} is a [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] located in the Saidiaji-Shiba neighborhood of the city of [[Nara, Nara|Nara]], [[Nara Prefecture]], Japan. It became the head temple of the {{nihongo|[[Shingon Risshu]]|真言律宗}} sect after the sect's founder, {{nihongo|[[Eison]]|叡尊}}, took over administration in 1238.{{cite book |last=Watt | first=Paul B. |editor-first=George| editor-last=Tanabe |title=Religions of Japan in Practice |publisher=Princeton University Press |date=March 8, 1999 |chapter=Chapter 7: Eison and the Shingon Vinaya Sect |isbn=0-691-05789-3}} The ''[[honzon]]'' of the temple is a statue of [[Shakyamuni|Shaka Nyorai]], enshrined by Eison in 1249. The temple was once one of the powerful [[Nanto Shichi Daiji|Seven Great Temples]] of the ancient capital of [[Heijō-kyō]]. ==History== According to the {{nihongo|''Saidaiji Zaizairyukicho''|西大寺資財流記帳|}} from 780, in September 764 when the retired [[Empress Koken]] requested the construction of a gilt bronze statue of the [[Four Heavenly Kings]] to pray for the end of the [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]]. In October of the same year, Empress Koken re-enthroned as [[Empress Shōtoku]]. The following year, in 765, the aforementioned statues of the Four Heavenly Kings were completed and Saidai-ji Temple was founded. These four statues are still enshrined in Saidai-ji's Shiōdō Hall, but only the demons that each statue steps on are from the time of the temple's founding, and the statues themselves have been replaced by later works. At the time of Saidai-ji's founding, the monk [[Dōkyō]] held great influence in the political world, and it is believed that Dōkyō's ideological influence was also great in the construction of Saidai-ji Temple. The temple's name of "Saidai-ji" is a counterpoint to the great national temple of [[Tōdai-ji]] and thus a challenge to the existing political and religious power structure. The temple in its initial form was a huge complex and was counted as one of the [[Nanto Shichi Daiji|Seven Great Temples of Nara]]. It has two Main Halls: the Yakushi Kondō and the Miroku Kondō, numerous smaller halls (including the Shio-do, Juichimen-do), and twin five-story pagodas on the east and west. According to the "Zaizairyuki-cho", these halls housed many Buddhist statues and were decorated with many mirrors. In the Miroku Kondō alone, a total of 77 Buddhist statues were placed, and the Yakushi Kondō housed 21 statues. However, the temple fell quickly into decline during the [[Heian period]], and many of its halls and pagodas were lost to fires and typhoons, and it came under the control of [[Kofuku-ji]]. The temple was restored in the [[Kamakura period]] by the monk [[Eison]]. He began the restoration in 1238 over a 50 years period worked to transform the temple into a center for social welfare work, especially to help the poor and sick. Many of the Buddhist statues and crafts that remain at the temple today were created during the time of Eison. The temple also produced many noteworthy priests, such as [[Ninsho]], who worked to restore ruined temples of various provinces, including several of the ''[[kokubunji]]'' temples. In the [[Muromachi period]], the temple was burned down in December 1499 during the wars of the early [[Sengoku period]], and its surviving East Pagoda was destroyed by a fire in 1502. During the [[Edo period]], the temple received a fief of 300 ''[[koku]]'' from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and began reconstruction. All of the current temple buildings were rebuilt after the Edo Period. Saidai-ji became independent from the [[Shingon]] sect in June 1895, and established the Shingon Ritsu sect. The temple houses numerous [[National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasures]] and [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]]. The precincts of Saidai-ji were designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1965. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2006|title=西大寺境内|trans-title= Saidai-ji keinai|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2022}} == Building list == *[[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] (Hondō – 本堂) – [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]. It was rebuilt in 1808. *Shiō-dō (四王堂) – It was rebuilt in 1674. *Aizen-dō (愛染堂) – It was reconstructed in 1762. File:Saidai-ji Nara Japan03n.jpg|Shiō-dō File:Saidai-ji5.JPG|Aizen-dō File:Kishi Monju pentad (Saidaiji Nara).jpg|[[Manjushri|Manjushri Bodhisattva]] (Monju Bosatsu) ==See also== * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts-others)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] * [[Nanto Shichi Daiji]], Seven Great Temples of Nanto. * [[Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato]] *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * {{cite web|title=Saidai-ji Temple Homepage| language=Japanese| url=http://www.naranet.co.jp/saidaiji/| accessdate = 2010-02-05}} {{Commons category-inline|Saidaiji (Nara)|Saidai-ji}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Saidai-Ji}} [[Category:8th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Nara, Nara]] [[Category:Shingon Ritsu temples]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:8th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Nara period]] [[Category:765 establishments]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in the 760s]] [[Category:Temples of Gautama Buddha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia44]] [[Category:Completed pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Saidai-ji]]

File:Saidai-ji Nara Japan03n.jpg|Shiō-dō File:Saidai-ji5.JPG|Aizen-dō File:Kishi Monju pentad (Saidaiji Nara).jpg|[[Manjushri|Manjushri Bodhisattva]] (Monju Bosatsu) ==See also== * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts-others)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] * [[Nanto Shichi Daiji]], Seven Great Temples of Nanto. * [[Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato]] *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * {{cite web|title=Saidai-ji Temple Homepage| language=Japanese| url=http://www.naranet.co.jp/saidaiji/| accessdate = 2010-02-05}} {{Commons category-inline|Saidaiji (Nara)|Saidai-ji}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Saidai-Ji}} [[Category:8th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Nara, Nara]] [[Category:Shingon Ritsu temples]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:8th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Nara period]] [[Category:765 establishments]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in the 760s]] [[Category:Temples of Gautama Buddha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Completed pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Saidai-ji]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Saidai-ji | native_name = 西大寺 | image = Saidai-ji Nara Japan08bs3.jpg | image_size = 300 | alt = | caption = [[Hondō|Main Hall]] | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Saidai-ji | location = 1-1-5 Saidaiji Shibachō, Nara-shi, Nara-ken | coordinates = {{coord|34|41|37|N|135|46|46.2|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = [[Buddhist]] | rite = [[Shingon Risshu|Shingon Ritsu]] | deity = [[Shaka Nyorai]] (Śākyamuni)
([[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple | website = {{url|http://saidaiji.or.jp/}} | founded_by = [[Jōtō (monk)|Jōtō]] and [[Empress Kōken]] |established=765| year_completed = 1808 (Reconstruction) | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|41|37|N|135|46|46.2|E}}}}} }} [[file:200730 Model of Saidaiji in Heijokyo viewed from north.jpg|thumb|right|280px|Model of Nara period Saidai-ji]] {{nihongo|'''Saidai-ji'''|西大寺}} is a [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] located in the Saidiaji-Shiba neighborhood of the city of [[Nara, Nara|Nara]], [[Nara Prefecture]], Japan. It became the head temple of the {{nihongo|[[Shingon Risshu]]|真言律宗}} sect after the sect's founder, {{nihongo|[[Eison]]|叡尊}}, took over administration in 1238.{{cite book |last=Watt | first=Paul B. |editor-first=George| editor-last=Tanabe |title=Religions of Japan in Practice |publisher=Princeton University Press |date=March 8, 1999 |chapter=Chapter 7: Eison and the Shingon Vinaya Sect |isbn=0-691-05789-3}} The ''[[honzon]]'' of the temple is a statue of [[Shakyamuni|Shaka Nyorai]], enshrined by Eison in 1249. The temple was once one of the powerful [[Nanto Shichi Daiji|Seven Great Temples]] of the ancient capital of [[Heijō-kyō]]. ==History== According to the {{nihongo|''Saidaiji Zaizairyukicho''|西大寺資財流記帳|}} from 780, in September 764 when the retired [[Empress Koken]] requested the construction of a gilt bronze statue of the [[Four Heavenly Kings]] to pray for the end of the [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]]. In October of the same year, Empress Koken re-enthroned as [[Empress Shōtoku]]. The following year, in 765, the aforementioned statues of the Four Heavenly Kings were completed and Saidai-ji Temple was founded. These four statues are still enshrined in Saidai-ji's Shiōdō Hall, but only the demons that each statue steps on are from the time of the temple's founding, and the statues themselves have been replaced by later works. At the time of Saidai-ji's founding, the monk [[Dōkyō]] held great influence in the political world, and it is believed that Dōkyō's ideological influence was also great in the construction of Saidai-ji Temple. The temple's name of "Saidai-ji" is a counterpoint to the great national temple of [[Tōdai-ji]] and thus a challenge to the existing political and religious power structure. The temple in its initial form was a huge complex and was counted as one of the [[Nanto Shichi Daiji|Seven Great Temples of Nara]]. It has two Main Halls: the Yakushi Kondō and the Miroku Kondō, numerous smaller halls (including the Shio-do, Juichimen-do), and twin five-story pagodas on the east and west. According to the "Zaizairyuki-cho", these halls housed many Buddhist statues and were decorated with many mirrors. In the Miroku Kondō alone, a total of 77 Buddhist statues were placed, and the Yakushi Kondō housed 21 statues. However, the temple fell quickly into decline during the [[Heian period]], and many of its halls and pagodas were lost to fires and typhoons, and it came under the control of [[Kofuku-ji]]. The temple was restored in the [[Kamakura period]] by the monk [[Eison]]. He began the restoration in 1238 over a 50 years period worked to transform the temple into a center for social welfare work, especially to help the poor and sick. Many of the Buddhist statues and crafts that remain at the temple today were created during the time of Eison. The temple also produced many noteworthy priests, such as [[Ninsho]], who worked to restore ruined temples of various provinces, including several of the ''[[kokubunji]]'' temples. In the [[Muromachi period]], the temple was burned down in December 1499 during the wars of the early [[Sengoku period]], and its surviving East Pagoda was destroyed by a fire in 1502. During the [[Edo period]], the temple received a fief of 300 ''[[koku]]'' from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and began reconstruction. All of the current temple buildings were rebuilt after the Edo Period. Saidai-ji became independent from the [[Shingon]] sect in June 1895, and established the Shingon Ritsu sect. The temple houses numerous [[National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasures]] and [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]]. The precincts of Saidai-ji were designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1965. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2006|title=西大寺境内|trans-title= Saidai-ji keinai|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2022}} == Building list == *[[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] (Hondō – 本堂) – [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]. It was rebuilt in 1808. *Shiō-dō (四王堂) – It was rebuilt in 1674. *Aizen-dō (愛染堂) – It was reconstructed in 1762. File:Saidai-ji Nara Japan03n.jpg|Shiō-dō File:Saidai-ji5.JPG|Aizen-dō File:Kishi Monju pentad (Saidaiji Nara).jpg|[[Manjushri|Manjushri Bodhisattva]] (Monju Bosatsu) ==See also== * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts-others)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] * [[Nanto Shichi Daiji]], Seven Great Temples of Nanto. * [[Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato]] *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * {{cite web|title=Saidai-ji Temple Homepage| language=Japanese| url=http://www.naranet.co.jp/saidaiji/| accessdate = 2010-02-05}} {{Commons category-inline|Saidaiji (Nara)|Saidai-ji}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Saidai-Ji}} [[Category:8th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Buddhist temples in Nara, Nara]] [[Category:Shingon Ritsu temples]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:8th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Nara period]] [[Category:765 establishments]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in the 760s]] [[Category:Temples of Gautama Buddha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Completed pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Saidai-ji]]

The temple's name of "Saidai-ji" is a counterpoint to the great national temple of [[Tōdai-ji]] and thus a challenge to the existing political and religious power structure. The temple in its initial form was a huge complex and was counted as one of the [[Nanto Shichi Daiji|Seven Great Temples of Nara]]. It has two Main Halls: the Yakushi Kondō and the Miroku Kondō, numerous smaller halls (including the Shio-do, Juichimen-do), and twin five-story pagodas on the east and west. According to the "Zaizairyuki-cho", these halls housed many Buddhist statues and were decorated with many mirrors. In the Miroku Kondō alone, a total of 77 Buddhist statues were placed, and the Yakushi Kondō housed 21 statues. However, the temple fell quickly into decline during the [[Heian period]], and many of its halls and pagodas were lost to fires and typhoons, and it came under the control of [[Kofuku-ji]]. The temple was restored in the [[Kamakura period]] by the monk [[Eison]]. He began the restoration in 1238 over a 50 years period worked to transform the temple into a center for social welfare work, especially to help the poor and sick. Many of the Buddhist statues and crafts that remain at the temple today were created during the time of Eison. The temple also produced many noteworthy priests, such as [[Ninsho]], who worked to restore ruined temples of various provinces, including several of the ''[[kokubunji]]'' temples. In the [[Muromachi period]], the temple was burned down in December 1499 during the wars of the early [[Sengoku period]], and its surviving East Pagoda was destroyed by a fire in 1502. During the [[Edo period]], the temple received a fief of 300 ''[[koku]]'' from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and began reconstruction. All of the current temple buildings were rebuilt after the Edo Period. Saidai-ji became independent from the [[Shingon]] sect in June 1895, and established the Shingon Ritsu sect. The temple houses numerous [[National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasures]] and [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]]. The precincts of Saidai-ji were designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1965. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2006|title=西大寺境内|trans-title= Saidai-ji keinai|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2022}} == Building list == *[[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] (Hondō – 本堂) – [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]. It was rebuilt in 1808. *Shiō-dō (四王堂) – It was rebuilt in 1674. *Aizen-dō (愛染堂) – It was reconstructed in 1762. File:Saidai-ji Nara Japan03n.jpg|Shiō-dō File:Saidai-ji5.JPG|Aizen-dō File:Kishi Monju pentad (Saidaiji Nara).jpg|[[Manjushri|Manjushri Bodhisattva]] (Monju Bosatsu) ==See also== * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts-others)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] * [[Nanto Shichi Daiji]], Seven Great Temples of Nanto. * [[Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato]] *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * {{cite {{Infobox religious building | name = Saidai-ji | native_name = 西大寺 | image = Saidai-ji Nara Japan08bs3.jpg | image_size = 300 | alt = | caption = [[Hondō|Main Hall]] | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Saidai-ji | location = 1-1-5 Saidaiji Shibachō, Nara-shi, Nara-ken | coordinates = {{coord|34|41|37|N|135|46|46.2|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = [[Buddhist]] | rite = [[Shingon Risshu|Shingon Ritsu]] | deity = [[Shaka Nyorai]] (Śākyamuni)
([[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple | website = {{url|http://saidaiji.or.jp/}} | founded_by = [[Jōtō (monk)|Jōtō]] and [[Empress Kōken]] |established=765| year_completed = 1808 (Reconstruction) | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|41|37|N|135|46|46.2|E}}}}} }} [[file:200730 Model of Saidaiji in Heijokyo viewed from north.jpg|thumb|right|280px|Model of Nara period Saidai-ji]] {{nihongo|'''Saidai-ji'''|西大寺}} is a [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] located in the Saidiaji-Shiba neighborhood of the city of [[Nara, Nara|Nara]], [[Nara Prefecture]], Japan. It became the head temple of the {{nihongo|[[Shingon Risshu]]|真言律宗}} sect after the sect's founder, {{nihongo|[[Eison]]|叡尊}}, took over administration in 1238.{{cite book |last=Watt | first=Paul B. |editor-first=George| editor-last=Tanabe |title=Religions of Japan in Practice |publisher=Princeton University Press |date=March 8, 1999 |chapter=Chapter 7: Eison and the Shingon Vinaya Sect |isbn=0-691-05789-3}} The ''[[honzon]]'' of the temple is a statue of [[Shakyamuni|Shaka Nyorai]], enshrined by Eison in 1249. The temple was once one of the powerful [[Nanto Shichi Daiji|Seven Great Temples]] of the ancient capital of [[Heijō-kyō]]. ==History== According to the {{nihongo|''Saidaiji Zaizairyukicho''|西大寺資財流記帳|}} from 780, in September 764 when the retired [[Empress Koken]] requested the construction of a gilt bronze statue of the [[Four Heavenly Kings]] to pray for the end of the [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]]. In October of the same year, Empress Koken re-enthroned as [[Empress Shōtoku]]. The following year, in 765, the aforementioned statues of the Four Heavenly Kings were completed and Saidai-ji Temple was founded. These four statues are still enshrined in Saidai-ji's Shiōdō Hall, but only the demons that each statue steps on are from the time of the temple's founding, and the statues themselves have been replaced by later works. At the time of Saidai-ji's founding, the monk [[Dōkyō]] held great influence in the political world, and it is believed that Dōkyō's ideological influence was also great in the construction of Saidai-ji Temple. The temple's name of "Saidai-ji" is a counterpoint to the great national temple of [[Tōdai-ji]] and thus a challenge to the existing political and religious power structure. The temple in its initial form was a huge complex and was counted as one of the [[Nanto Shichi Daiji|Seven Great Temples of Nara]]. It has two Main Halls: the Yakushi Kondō and the Miroku Kondō, numerous smaller halls (including the Shio-do, Juichimen-do), and twin five-story pagodas on the east and west. According to the "Zaizairyuki-cho", these halls housed many Buddhist statues and were decorated with many mirrors. In the Miroku Kondō alone, a total of 77 Buddhist statues were placed, and the Yakushi Kondō housed 21 statues. However, the temple fell quickly into decline during the [[Heian period]], and many of its halls and pagodas were lost to fires and typhoons, and it came under the control of [[Kofuku-ji]]. The temple was restored in the [[Kamakura period]] by the monk [[Eison]]. He began the restoration in 1238 over a 50 years period worked to transform the temple into a center for social welfare work, especially to help the poor and sick. Many of the Buddhist statues and crafts that remain at the temple today were created during the time of Eison. The temple also produced many noteworthy priests, such as [[Ninsho]], who worked to restore ruined temples of various provinces, including several of the ''[[kokubunji]]'' temples. In the [[Muromachi period]], the temple was burned down in December 1499 during the wars of the early [[Sengoku period]], and its surviving East Pagoda was destroyed by a fire in 1502. During the [[Edo period]], the temple received a fief of 300 ''[[koku]]'' from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and began reconstruction. All of the current temple buildings were rebuilt after the Edo Period. Saidai-ji became independent from the [[Shingon]] sect in June 1895, and established the Shingon Ritsu sect. The temple houses numerous [[National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasures]] and [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]]. The precincts of Saidai-ji were designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1965. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2006|title=西大寺境内|trans-title= Saidai-ji keinai|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2022}} == Building list == *[[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] (Hondō – 本堂) – [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]. It was rebuilt in 1808. *Shiō-dō (四王堂) – It was rebuilt in 1674. *Aizen-dō (愛染堂) – It was reconstructed in 1762. File:Saidai-ji Nara Japan03n.jpg|Shiō-dō File:Saidai-ji5.JPG|Aizen-dō File:Kishi Monju pentad (Saidaiji Nara).jpg|[[Manjushri|Manjushri Bodhisattva]] (Monju Bosatsu) ==See also== * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts-others)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] * [[Nanto Shichi Daiji]], Seven Great Temples of Nanto. * [[Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato]] *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * {{cite revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Saidai-ji | native_name = 西大寺 | image = Saidai-ji Nara Japan08bs3.jpg | image_size = 300 | alt = | caption = [[Hondō|Main Hall]] | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Saidai-ji | location = 1-1-5 Saidaiji Shibachō, Nara-shi, Nara-ken | coordinates = {{coord|34|41|37|N|135|46|46.2|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = [[Buddhist]] | rite = [[Shingon Risshu|Shingon Ritsu]] | deity = [[Shaka Nyorai]] (Śākyamuni)
([[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple | website = {{url|http://saidaiji.or.jp/}} | founded_by = [[Jōtō (monk)|Jōtō]] and [[Empress Kōken]] |established=765| year_completed = 1808 (Reconstruction) | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|41|37|N|135|46|46.2|E}}}}} }} [[file:200730 Model of Saidaiji in Heijokyo viewed from north.jpg|thumb|right|280px|Model of Nara period Saidai-ji]] {{nihongo|'''Saidai-ji'''|西大寺}} is a [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] located in the Saidiaji-Shiba neighborhood of the city of [[Nara, Nara|Nara]], [[Nara Prefecture]], Japan. It became the head temple of the {{nihongo|[[Shingon Risshu]]|真言律宗}} sect after the sect's founder, {{nihongo|[[Eison]]|叡尊}}, took over administration in 1238.{{cite book |last=Watt | first=Paul B. |editor-first=George| editor-last=Tanabe |title=Religions of Japan in Practice |publisher=Princeton University Press |date=March 8, 1999 |chapter=Chapter 7: Eison and the Shingon Vinaya Sect |isbn=0-691-05789-3}} The ''[[honzon]]'' of the temple is a statue of [[Shakyamuni|Shaka Nyorai]], enshrined by Eison in 1249. The temple was once one of the powerful [[Nanto Shichi Daiji|Seven Great Temples]] of the ancient capital of [[Heijō-kyō]]. ==History== According to the {{nihongo|''Saidaiji Zaizairyukicho''|西大寺資財流記帳|}} from 780, in September 764 when the retired [[Empress Koken]] requested the construction of a gilt bronze statue of the [[Four Heavenly Kings]] to pray for the end of the [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]]. In October of the same year, Empress Koken re-enthroned as [[Empress Shōtoku]]. The following year, in 765, the aforementioned statues of the Four Heavenly Kings were completed and Saidai-ji Temple was founded. These four statues are still enshrined in Saidai-ji's Shiōdō Hall, but only the demons that each statue steps on are from the time of the temple's founding, and the statues themselves have been replaced by later works. At the time of Saidai-ji's founding, the monk [[Dōkyō]] held great influence in the political world, and it is believed that Dōkyō's ideological influence was also great in the construction of Saidai-ji Temple. The temple's name of "Saidai-ji" is a counterpoint to the great national temple of [[Tōdai-ji]] and thus a challenge to the existing political and religious power structure. The temple in its initial form was a huge complex and was counted as one of the [[Nanto Shichi Daiji|Seven Great Temples of Nara]]. It has two Main Halls: the Yakushi Kondō and the Miroku Kondō, numerous smaller halls (including the Shio-do, Juichimen-do), and twin five-story pagodas on the east and west. According to the "Zaizairyuki-cho", these halls housed many Buddhist statues and were decorated with many mirrors. In the Miroku Kondō alone, a total of 77 Buddhist statues were placed, and the Yakushi Kondō housed 21 statues. However, the temple fell quickly into decline during the [[Heian period]], and many of its halls and pagodas were lost to fires and typhoons, and it came under the control of [[Kofuku-ji]]. The temple was restored in the [[Kamakura period]] by the monk [[Eison]]. He began the restoration in 1238 over a 50 years period worked to transform the temple into a center for social welfare work, especially to help the poor and sick. Many of the Buddhist statues and crafts that remain at the temple today were created during the time of Eison. The temple also produced many noteworthy priests, such as [[Ninsho]], who worked to restore ruined temples of various provinces, including several of the ''[[kokubunji]]'' temples. In the [[Muromachi period]], the temple was burned down in December 1499 during the wars of the early [[Sengoku period]], and its surviving East Pagoda was destroyed by a fire in 1502. During the [[Edo period]], the temple received a fief of 300 ''[[koku]]'' from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and began reconstruction. All of the current temple buildings were rebuilt after the Edo Period. Saidai-ji became independent from the [[Shingon]] sect in June 1895, and established the Shingon Ritsu sect. The temple houses numerous [[National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasures]] and [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]]. The precincts of Saidai-ji were designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1965. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2006|title=西大寺境内|trans-title= Saidai-ji keinai|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2022}} == Building list == *[[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] (Hondō – 本堂) – [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]. It was rebuilt in 1808. *Shiō-dō (四王堂) – It was rebuilt in 1674. *Aizen-dō (愛染堂) – It was reconstructed in 1762. File:Saidai-ji Nara Japan03n.jpg|Shiō-dō File:Saidai-ji5.JPG|Aizen-dō File:Kishi Monju pentad (Saidaiji Nara).jpg|[[Manjushri|Manjushri Bodhisattva]] (Monju Bosatsu) ==See also== * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts-others)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] * [[Nanto Shichi Daiji]], Seven Great Temples of Nanto. * [[Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato]] *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * {{cite web|title=Saidai-ji Temple Homepage| language=Japanese| url=http://www.naranet.co.jp/saidaiji/| accessdate = 2010-02-05}} {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category-inline|Saidaiji (Nara)|Saidai-ji}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Saidai-Ji}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:8th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Buddhist temples in Nara, Nara]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Shingon Ritsu temples]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:8th-century establishments in Japan]] {{Infobox religious building | name = Saidai-ji | native_name = 西大寺 | image = Saidai-ji Nara Japan08bs3.jpg | image_size = 300 | alt = | caption = [[Hondō|Main Hall]] | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Saidai-ji | location = 1-1-5 Saidaiji Shibachō, Nara-shi, Nara-ken | coordinates = {{coord|34|41|37|N|135|46|46.2|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = [[Buddhist]] | rite = [[Shingon Risshu|Shingon Ritsu]] | deity = [[Shaka Nyorai]] (Śākyamuni)
([[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple | website = {{url|http://saidaiji.or.jp/}} | founded_by = [[Jōtō (monk)|Jōtō]] and [[Empress Kōken]] |established=765| year_completed = 1808 (Reconstruction) | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|41|37|N|135|46|46.2|E}}}}} }} [[file:200730 Model of Saidaiji in Heijokyo viewed from north.jpg|thumb|right|280px|Model of Nara period Saidai-ji]] {{nihongo|'''Saidai-ji'''|西大寺}} is a [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] located in the Saidiaji-Shiba neighborhood of the city of [[Nara, Nara|Nara]], [[Nara Prefecture]], Japan. It became the head temple of the {{nihongo|[[Shingon Risshu]]|真言律宗}} sect after the sect's founder, {{nihongo|[[Eison]]|叡尊}}, took over administration in 1238.{{cite book |last=Watt | first=Paul B. |editor-first=George| editor-last=Tanabe |title=Religions of Japan in Practice |publisher=Princeton University Press |date=March 8, 1999 |chapter=Chapter 7: Eison and the Shingon Vinaya Sect |isbn=0-691-05789-3}} The ''[[honzon]]'' of the temple is a statue of [[Shakyamuni|Shaka Nyorai]], enshrined by Eison in 1249. The temple was once one of the powerful [[Nanto Shichi Daiji|Seven Great Temples]] of the ancient capital of [[Heijō-kyō]]. ==History== According to the {{nihongo|''Saidaiji Zaizairyukicho''|西大寺資財流記帳|}} from 780, in September 764 when the retired [[Empress Koken]] requested the construction of a gilt bronze statue of the [[Four Heavenly Kings]] to pray for the end of the [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]]. In October of the same year, Empress Koken re-enthroned as [[Empress Shōtoku]]. The following year, in 765, the aforementioned statues of the Four Heavenly Kings were completed and Saidai-ji Temple was founded. These four statues are still enshrined in Saidai-ji's Shiōdō Hall, but only the demons that each statue steps on are from the time of the temple's founding, and the statues themselves have been replaced by later works. At the time of Saidai-ji's founding, the monk [[Dōkyō]] held great influence in the political world, and it is believed that Dōkyō's ideological influence was also great in the construction of Saidai-ji Temple. The temple's name of "Saidai-ji" is a counterpoint to the great national temple of [[Tōdai-ji]] and thus a challenge to the existing political and religious power structure. The temple in its initial form was a huge complex and was counted as one of the [[Nanto Shichi Daiji|Seven Great Temples of Nara]]. It has two Main Halls: the Yakushi Kondō and the Miroku Kondō, numerous smaller halls (including the Shio-do, Juichimen-do), and twin five-story pagodas on the east and west. According to the "Zaizairyuki-cho", these halls housed many Buddhist statues and were decorated with many mirrors. In the Miroku Kondō alone, a total of 77 Buddhist statues were placed, and the Yakushi Kondō housed 21 statues. However, the temple fell quickly into decline during the [[Heian period]], and many of its halls and pagodas were lost to fires and typhoons, and it came under the control of [[Kofuku-ji]]. The temple was restored in the [[Kamakura period]] by the monk [[Eison]]. He began the restoration in 1238 over a 50 years period worked to transform the temple into a center for social welfare work, especially to help the poor and sick. Many of the Buddhist statues and crafts that remain at the temple today were created during the time of Eison. The temple also produced many noteworthy priests, such as [[Ninsho]], who worked to restore ruined temples of various provinces, including several of the ''[[kokubunji]]'' temples. In the [[Muromachi period]], the temple was burned down in December 1499 during the wars of the early [[Sengoku period]], and its surviving East Pagoda was destroyed by a fire in 1502. During the [[Edo period]], the temple received a fief of 300 ''[[koku]]'' from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and began reconstruction. All of the current temple buildings were rebuilt after the Edo Period. Saidai-ji became independent from the [[Shingon]] sect in June 1895, and established the Shingon Ritsu sect. The temple houses numerous [[National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasures]] and [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]]. The precincts of Saidai-ji were designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1965. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2006|title=西大寺境内|trans-title= Saidai-ji keinai|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2022}} == Building list == *[[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] (Hondō – 本堂) – [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]. It was rebuilt in 1808. *Shiō-dō (四王堂) – It was rebuilt in 1674. *Aizen-dō (愛染堂) – It was reconstructed in 1762. File:Saidai-ji Nara Japan03n.jpg|Shiō-dō File:Saidai-ji5.JPG|Aizen-dō File:Kishi Monju pentad (Saidaiji Nara).jpg|[[Manjushri|Manjushri Bodhisattva]] (Monju Bosatsu) ==See also== * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts-others)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] * [[Nanto Shichi Daiji]], Seven Great Temples of Nanto. * [[Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato]] *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * {{cite web|title=Saidai-ji Temple Homepage| language=Japanese| url=http://www.naranet.co.jp/saidaiji/| accessdate = 2010-02-05}} {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category-inline|Saidaiji (Nara)|Saidai-ji}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Authority control}} Nara, Nara]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Shingon Ritsu temples]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] ([[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple | website = {{url|http://saidaiji.or.jp/}} | founded_by = [[Jōtō (monk)|Jōtō]] and [[Empress Kōken]] |established=765| year_completed = 1808 (Reconstruction) | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|41|37|N|135|46|46.2|E}}}}} }} [[file:200730 Model of Saidaiji in Heijokyo viewed from north.jpg|thumb|right|280px|Model of Nara period Saidai-ji]] {{nihongo|'''Saidai-ji'''|西大寺}} is a [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] located in the Saidiaji-Shiba neighborhood of the city of [[Nara, Nara|Nara]], [[Nara Prefecture]], Japan. It became the head temple of the {{nihongo|[[Shingon Risshu]]|真言律宗}} sect after the sect's founder, {{nihongo|[[Eison]]|叡尊}}, took over administration in 1238.{{cite book |last=Watt | first=Paul B. |editor-first=George| editor-last=Tanabe |title=Religions of Japan in Practice |publisher=Princeton University Press |date=March 8, 1999 |chapter=Chapter 7: Eison and the Shingon Vinaya Sect |isbn=0-691-05789-3}} The ''[[honzon]]'' of the temple is a statue of [[Shakyamuni|Shaka Nyorai]], enshrined by Eison in 1249. The temple was once one of the powerful [[Nanto Shichi Daiji|Seven Great Temples]] of the ancient capital of [[Heijō-kyō]]. ==History== According to the {{nihongo|''Saidaiji Zaizairyukicho''|西大寺資財流記帳|}} from 780, in September 764 when the retired [[Empress Koken]] requested the construction of a gilt bronze statue of the [[Four Heavenly Kings]] to pray for the end of the [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]]. In October of the same year, Empress Koken re-enthroned as [[Empress Shōtoku]]. The following year, in 765, the aforementioned statues of the Four Heavenly Kings were completed and Saidai-ji Temple was founded. These four statues are still enshrined in Saidai-ji's Shiōdō Hall, but only the demons that each statue steps on are from the time of the temple's founding, and the statues themselves have been replaced by later works. At the time of Saidai-ji's founding, the monk [[Dōkyō]] held great influence in the political world, and it is believed that Dōkyō's ideological influence was also great in the construction of Saidai-ji Temple. The temple's name of "Saidai-ji" is a counterpoint to the great national temple of [[Tōdai-ji]] and thus a challenge to the existing political and religious power structure. The temple in its initial form was a huge complex and was counted as one of the [[Nanto Shichi Daiji|Seven Great Temples of Nara]]. It has two Main Halls: the Yakushi Kondō and the Miroku Kondō, numerous smaller halls (including the Shio-do, Juichimen-do), and twin five-story pagodas on the east and west. According to the "Zaizairyuki-cho", these halls housed many Buddhist statues and were decorated with many mirrors. In the Miroku Kondō alone, a total of 77 Buddhist statues were placed, and the Yakushi Kondō housed 21 statues. However, the temple fell quickly into decline during the [[Heian period]], and many of its halls and pagodas were lost to fires and typhoons, and it came under the control of [[Kofuku-ji]]. The temple was restored in the [[Kamakura period]] by the monk [[Eison]]. He began the restoration in 1238 over a 50 years period worked to transform the temple into a center for social welfare work, especially to help the poor and sick. Many of the Buddhist statues and crafts that remain at the temple today were created during the time of Eison. The temple also produced many noteworthy priests, such as [[Ninsho]], who worked to restore ruined temples of various provinces, including several of the ''[[kokubunji]]'' temples. In the [[Muromachi period]], the temple was burned down in December 1499 during the wars of the early [[Sengoku period]], and its surviving East Pagoda was destroyed by a fire in 1502. During the [[Edo period]], the temple received a fief of 300 ''[[koku]]'' from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and began reconstruction. All of the current temple buildings were rebuilt after the Edo Period. Saidai-ji became independent from the [[Shingon]] sect in June 1895, and established the Shingon Ritsu sect. The temple houses numerous [[National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasures]] and [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]]. The precincts of Saidai-ji were designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1965. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2006|title=西大寺境内|trans-title= Saidai-ji keinai|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2022}} == Building list == *[[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] (Hondō – 本堂) – [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]. It was rebuilt in 1808. *Shiō-dō (四王堂) – It was rebuilt in 1674. *Aizen-dō (愛染堂) – It was reconstructed in 1762. File:Saidai-ji Nara Japan03n.jpg|Shiō-dō File:Saidai-ji5.JPG|Aizen-dō File:Kishi Monju pentad (Saidaiji Nara).jpg|[[Manjushri|Manjushri Bodhisattva]] (Monju Bosatsu) ==See also== * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts-others)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] * [[Nanto Shichi Daiji]], Seven Great Temples of Nanto. * [[Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato]] *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * {{cite web|title=Saidai-ji Temple Homepage| language=Japanese| url=http://www.naranet.co.jp/saidaiji/| accessdate = 2010-02-05}} {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and {{Infobox religious building | name = Saidai-ji | native_name = 西大寺 | image = Saidai-ji Nara Japan08bs3.jpg | image_size = 300 | alt = | caption = [[Hondō|Main Hall]] | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Saidai-ji | location = 1-1-5 Saidaiji Shibachō, Nara-shi, Nara-ken | coordinates = {{coord|34|41|37|N|135|46|46.2|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = [[Buddhist]] | rite = [[Shingon Risshu|Shingon Ritsu]] | deity = [[Shaka Nyorai]] (Śākyamuni)
([[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple | website = {{url|http://saidaiji.or.jp/}} | founded_by = [[Jōtō (monk)|Jōtō]] and [[Empress Kōken]] |established=765| year_completed = 1808 (Reconstruction) | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|41|37|N|135|46|46.2|E}}}}} }} [[file:200730 Model of Saidaiji in Heijokyo viewed from north.jpg|thumb|right|280px|Model of Nara period Saidai-ji]] {{nihongo|'''Saidai-ji'''|西大寺}} is a [[Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] located in the Saidiaji-Shiba neighborhood of the city of [[Nara, Nara|Nara]], [[Nara Prefecture]], Japan. It became the head temple of the {{nihongo|[[Shingon Risshu]]|真言律宗}} sect after the sect's founder, {{nihongo|[[Eison]]|叡尊}}, took over administration in 1238.{{cite book |last=Watt | first=Paul B. |editor-first=George| editor-last=Tanabe |title=Religions of Japan in Practice |publisher=Princeton University Press |date=March 8, 1999 |chapter=Chapter 7: Eison and the Shingon Vinaya Sect |isbn=0-691-05789-3}} The ''[[honzon]]'' of the temple is a statue of [[Shakyamuni|Shaka Nyorai]], enshrined by Eison in 1249. The temple was once one of the powerful [[Nanto Shichi Daiji|Seven Great Temples]] of the ancient capital of [[Heijō-kyō]]. ==History== According to the {{nihongo|''Saidaiji Zaizairyukicho''|西大寺資財流記帳|}} from 780, in September 764 when the retired [[Empress Koken]] requested the construction of a gilt bronze statue of the [[Four Heavenly Kings]] to pray for the end of the [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]]. In October of the same year, Empress Koken re-enthroned as [[Empress Shōtoku]]. The following year, in 765, the aforementioned statues of the Four Heavenly Kings were completed and Saidai-ji Temple was founded. These four statues are still enshrined in Saidai-ji's Shiōdō Hall, but only the demons that each statue steps on are from the time of the temple's founding, and the statues themselves have been replaced by later works. At the time of Saidai-ji's founding, the monk [[Dōkyō]] held great influence in the political world, and it is believed that Dōkyō's ideological influence was also great in the construction of Saidai-ji Temple. The temple's name of "Saidai-ji" is a counterpoint to the great national temple of [[Tōdai-ji]] and thus a challenge to the existing political and religious power structure. The temple in its initial form was a huge complex and was counted as one of the [[Nanto Shichi Daiji|Seven Great Temples of Nara]]. It has two Main Halls: the Yakushi Kondō and the Miroku Kondō, numerous smaller halls (including the Shio-do, Juichimen-do), and twin five-story pagodas on the east and west. According to the "Zaizairyuki-cho", these halls housed many Buddhist statues and were decorated with many mirrors. In the Miroku Kondō alone, a total of 77 Buddhist statues were placed, and the Yakushi Kondō housed 21 statues. However, the temple fell quickly into decline during the [[Heian period]], and many of its halls and pagodas were lost to fires and typhoons, and it came under the control of [[Kofuku-ji]]. The temple was restored in the [[Kamakura period]] by the monk [[Eison]]. He began the restoration in 1238 over a 50 years period worked to transform the temple into a center for social welfare work, especially to help the poor and sick. Many of the Buddhist statues and crafts that remain at the temple today were created during the time of Eison. The temple also produced many noteworthy priests, such as [[Ninsho]], who worked to restore ruined temples of various provinces, including several of the ''[[kokubunji]]'' temples. In the [[Muromachi period]], the temple was burned down in December 1499 during the wars of the early [[Sengoku period]], and its surviving East Pagoda was destroyed by a fire in 1502. During the [[Edo period]], the temple received a fief of 300 ''[[koku]]'' from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and began reconstruction. All of the current temple buildings were rebuilt after the Edo Period. Saidai-ji became independent from the [[Shingon]] sect in June 1895, and established the Shingon Ritsu sect. The temple houses numerous [[National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasures]] and [[Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]]. The precincts of Saidai-ji were designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1965. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2006|title=西大寺境内|trans-title= Saidai-ji keinai|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2022}} == Building list == *[[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] (Hondō – 本堂) – [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]. It was rebuilt in 1808. *Shiō-dō (四王堂) – It was rebuilt in 1674. *Aizen-dō (愛染堂) – It was reconstructed in 1762. File:Saidai-ji Nara Japan03n.jpg|Shiō-dō File:Saidai-ji5.JPG|Aizen-dō File:Kishi Monju pentad (Saidaiji Nara).jpg|[[Manjushri|Manjushri Bodhisattva]] (Monju Bosatsu) ==See also== * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts-others)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)]] * [[List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] * [[Nanto Shichi Daiji]], Seven Great Temples of Nanto. * [[Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato]] *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * {{cite web|title=Saidai-ji Temple Homepage| language=Japanese| url=http://www.naranet.co.jp/saidaiji/| accessdate = 2010-02-05}} {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Saidai-ji]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Saidai-ji]][[Category:kk]]
