[[arz:سيداى جى]][[co:commons:Category:Saidaiji (Nara)]][[cs:Saidaidži]][[de:Saidai-ji]][[en:Saidai-ji]][[es:Saidai-ji]][[fr:Saidai-ji]][[it:Saidai-ji]][[ja:西大寺 (奈良市)]][[ko:사이다이지]][[no:Saidai-ji]][[pt:Saidai-ji]][[zh:西大寺 (奈良市)]] [[arz:سيداى جى]][[co:commons:Category:Saidaiji (Nara)]][[cs:Saidaidži]][[de:Saidai-ji]][[en:Saidai-ji]][[es:Saidai-ji]][[fr:Saidai-ji]][[it:Saidai-ji]][[ja:西大寺 (奈良市)]][[ko:사이다이지]][[no:Saidai-ji]][[pt:Saidai-ji]][[zh:西大寺 (奈良市)]] [[arz:سيداى جى]][[sn:commons:Category:Saidaiji (Nara)]][[cs:Saidaidži]][[de:Saidai-ji]][[en:Saidai-ji]][[es:Saidai-ji]][[fr:Saidai-ji]][[it:Saidai-ji]][[ja:西大寺 (奈良市)]][[ko:사이다이지]][[no:Saidai-ji]][[pt:Saidai-ji]][[zh:西大寺 (奈良市)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Saidai-ji Nara Japan08bs3.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shingon Risshu|WD=Q580527}}|prefecture={{ill|Nara|WD=Q169134}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.69361634|N|135.77949822|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Jōtō|WD=Q11481733}}; {{ill|Kōken|WD=Q232955}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537}}|part_of={{ill|Nanto Shichi Daiji|WD=Q1131210}}; {{ill|Q11627440|WD=Q11627440}}; {{ill|Q22120745|WD=Q22120745}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}}; {{ill|Shingon sect eighteen Motoyama|WD=Q10437732}}; {{ill|Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato|WD=Q11433417}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1203935]] Wikidata name: Saidai-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Saidai-ji | native_name = 西大寺 | image = Saidai-ji Nara Japan08bs3.jpg | image_size = 300 | alt = | caption = [[:en:Hondō|Main Hall]] | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Saidai-ji | location = 1-1-5 Saidaiji Shibachō, Nara-shi, Nara-ken | coordinates = {{coord|34|41|37|N|135|46|46.2|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] | rite = [[:en:Shingon Risshu|Shingon Ritsu]] | deity = [[:en:Shaka Nyorai|Shaka Nyorai]] (Shakyamuni Tathāgata)
([[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple | website = {{url|http://saidaiji.or.jp/}} | founded_by = [[Jōtō (monk)|Jōtō]] and [[:en:Empress Kōken|Empress Kōken]] |established=765| year_completed = 1808 (Reconstruction) | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|41|37|N|135|46|46.2|E}}}}} }} {{nihongo|'''Saidai-ji'''|西大寺}} is a [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temple]] located in the Saidiaji-Shiba neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]], Japan. It became the head temple of the {{nihongo|[[:en:Shingon Risshu|Shingon Risshu]]|真言律宗}} sect after the sect's founder, {{nihongo|[[:en:Eison|Eison]]|叡尊}}, took over administration in 1238.{{cite book |last=Watt | first=Paul B. |editor-first=George| editor-last=Tanabe |title=Religions of Japan in Practice |publisher=Princeton University Press |date=March 8, 1999 |chapter=Chapter 7: Eison and the Shingon Vinaya Sect |isbn=0-691-05789-3}} The ''[[honzon]]'' of the temple is a statue of [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata|Shaka Nyorai]], enshrined by Eison in 1249. The temple was once one of the powerful [[:en:Nanto Shichi Daiji|Seven Great Temples]] of the ancient capital of [[Heijō-kyō]]. ==History== According to the {{nihongo|''Saidaiji Zaizairyukicho''|西大寺資財流記帳|}} from 780, in September 764 when the retired [[:en:Empress Koken|Empress Koken]] requested the construction of a gilt bronze statue of the [[Four Heavenly Kings]] to pray for the end of the [[:en:Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion]]. In October of the same year, Empress Koken re-enthroned as [[:en:Empress Shōtoku|Empress Shōtoku]]. The following year, in 765, the aforementioned statues of the Four Heavenly Kings were completed and Saidai-ji Temple was founded. These four statues are still enshrined in Saidai-ji's Shiōdō Hall, but only the demons that each statue steps on are from the time of the temple's founding, and the statues themselves have been replaced by later works. At the time of Saidai-ji's founding, the monk [[:en:Dōkyō|Dōkyō]] held great influence in the political world, and it is believed that Dōkyō's ideological influence was also great in the construction of Saidai-ji Temple. The temple's name of "Saidai-ji" is a counterpoint to the great national temple of [[Tōdai-ji]] and thus a challenge to the existing political and religious power structure. The temple in its initial form was a huge complex and was counted as one of the [[:en:Nanto Shichi Daiji|Seven Great Temples of Nara]]. It has two Main Halls: the Yakushi Kondō and the Miroku Kondō, numerous smaller halls (including the Shio-do, Juichimen-do), and twin five-story pagodas on the east and west. According to the "Zaizairyuki-cho", these halls housed many Buddhist statues and were decorated with many mirrors. In the Miroku Kondō alone, a total of 77 Buddhist statues were placed, and the Yakushi Kondō housed 21 statues. However, the temple fell quickly into decline during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and many of its halls and pagodas were lost to fires and typhoons, and it came under the control of [[:en:Kofuku-ji|Kofuku-ji]]. The temple was restored in the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] by the monk [[:en:Eison|Eison]]. He began the restoration in 1238 over a 50 years period worked to transform the temple into a center for social welfare work, especially to help the poor and sick. Many of the Buddhist statues and crafts that remain at the temple today were created during the time of Eison. The temple also produced many noteworthy priests, such as [[:en:Ninsho|Ninsho]], who worked to restore ruined temples of various provinces, including several of the ''[[:en:Provincial temple|Provincial temple]]'' temples. In the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the temple was burned down in December 1499 during the wars of the early [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], and its surviving East Pagoda was destroyed by a fire in 1502. During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the temple received a fief of 300 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]'' from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and began reconstruction. All of the current temple buildings were rebuilt after the Edo Period. Saidai-ji became independent from the [[Shingon]] sect in June 1895, and established the Shingon Ritsu sect. The temple houses numerous [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasures]] and [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]]. The precincts of Saidai-ji were designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1965. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2006|title=西大寺境内|trans-title= Saidai-ji keinai|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2022}} == Building list == *[[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] (Hondō – 本堂) – [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Property]]. It was rebuilt in 1808. *Shiō-dō (四王堂) – It was rebuilt in 1674. *Aizen-dō (愛染堂) – It was reconstructed in 1762. File:Saidai-ji Nara Japan03n.jpg|Shiō-dō File:Saidai-ji5.JPG|Aizen-dō File:Kishi Monju pentad (Saidaiji Nara).jpg|[[Manjushri|Manjushri Bodhisattva]] (Monju Bosatsu) ==See also== * [[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)]] * [[:en:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] * [[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)|List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)]] * [[:en:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] * [[:en:Nanto Shichi Daiji|Nanto Shichi Daiji]], Seven Great Temples of Nanto. * [[:en:Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato|Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato]] *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * {{cite web|title=Saidai-ji Temple Homepage| language=Japanese| url=http://www.naranet.co.jp/saidaiji/| accessdate = 2010-02-05}} {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category-inline|Saidaiji (Nara)|Saidai-ji}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} [[arz:سيداى جى]][[co:commons:Category:Saidaiji (Nara)]][[cs:Saidaidži]][[de:Saidai-ji]][[en:Saidai-ji]][[es:Saidai-ji]][[fr:Saidai-ji]][[it:Saidai-ji]][[ja:西大寺 (奈良市)]][[ko:사이다이지]][[no:Saidai-ji]][[pt:Saidai-ji]][[zh:西大寺 (奈良市)]] [[arz:سيداى جى]][[co:commons:Category:Saidaiji (Nara)]][[cs:Saidaidži]][[de:Saidai-ji]][[en:Saidai-ji]][[es:Saidai-ji]][[fr:Saidai-ji]][[it:Saidai-ji]][[ja:西大寺 (奈良市)]][[ko:사이다이지]][[no:Saidai-ji]][[pt:Saidai-ji]][[zh:西大寺 (奈良市)]] [[arz:سيداى جى]][[sn:commons:Category:Saidaiji (Nara)]][[cs:Saidaidži]][[de:Saidai-ji]][[en:Saidai-ji]][[es:Saidai-ji]][[fr:Saidai-ji]][[it:Saidai-ji]][[ja:西大寺 (奈良市)]][[ko:사이다이지]][[no:Saidai-ji]][[pt:Saidai-ji]][[zh:西大寺 (奈良市)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Saidai-ji Nara Japan08bs3.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shingon Risshu|WD=Q580527}}|prefecture={{ill|Nara|WD=Q169134}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.69361634|N|135.77949822|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Jōtō|WD=Q11481733}}; {{ill|Kōken|WD=Q232955}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537}}|part_of={{ill|Nanto Shichi Daiji|WD=Q1131210}}; {{ill|Q11627440|WD=Q11627440}}; {{ill|Q22120745|WD=Q22120745}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}}; {{ill|Shingon sect eighteen Motoyama|WD=Q10437732}}; {{ill|Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato|WD=Q11433417}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1203935]] Wikidata name: Saidai-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Saidai-ji | native_name = 西大寺 | image = Saidai-ji Nara Japan08bs3.jpg | image_size = 300 | alt = | caption = [[:en:Hondō|Main Hall]] | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Saidai-ji | location = 1-1-5 Saidaiji Shibachō, Nara-shi, Nara-ken | coordinates = {{coord|34|41|37|N|135|46|46.2|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}} | rite = {{ill|Shingon Risshu|en|Shingon Risshu|WD=Q580527|lt=Shingon Ritsu}} | deity = [[:en:Shaka Nyorai|Shaka Nyorai]] (Shakyamuni Tathāgata)
({{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}}) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple | website = {{url|http://saidaiji.or.jp/}} | founded_by = [[Jōtō (monk)|Jōtō]] and {{ill|Empress Kōken|en|Empress Kōken|WD=Q232955|lt=Empress Kōken}} |established=765| year_completed = 1808 (Reconstruction) | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|41|37|N|135|46|46.2|E}}}}} }} {{nihongo|'''Saidai-ji'''|西大寺}} is a {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temple}} located in the Saidiaji-Shiba neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture}}, Japan. It became the head temple of the {{nihongo|{{ill|Shingon Risshu|en|Shingon Risshu|WD=Q580527|lt=Shingon Risshu}}|真言律宗}} sect after the sect's founder, {{nihongo|{{ill|Eison|en|Eison|WD=Q11410722|lt=Eison}}|叡尊}}, took over administration in 1238.{{cite book |last=Watt | first=Paul B. |editor-first=George| editor-last=Tanabe |title=Religions of Japan in Practice |publisher=Princeton University Press |date=March 8, 1999 |chapter=Chapter 7: Eison and the Shingon Vinaya Sect |isbn=0-691-05789-3}} The ''[[honzon]]'' of the temple is a statue of [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata|Shaka Nyorai]], enshrined by Eison in 1249. The temple was once one of the powerful {{ill|Nanto Shichi Daiji|en|Nanto Shichi Daiji|WD=Q1131210|lt=Seven Great Temples}} of the ancient capital of [[Heijō-kyō]]. ==History== According to the {{nihongo|''Saidaiji Zaizairyukicho''|西大寺資財流記帳|}} from 780, in September 764 when the retired [[:en:Empress Koken|Empress Koken]] requested the construction of a gilt bronze statue of the [[Four Heavenly Kings]] to pray for the end of the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|en|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|WD=Q11623017|lt=Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion}}. In October of the same year, Empress Koken re-enthroned as [[:en:Empress Shōtoku|Empress Shōtoku]]. The following year, in 765, the aforementioned statues of the Four Heavenly Kings were completed and Saidai-ji Temple was founded. These four statues are still enshrined in Saidai-ji's Shiōdō Hall, but only the demons that each statue steps on are from the time of the temple's founding, and the statues themselves have been replaced by later works. At the time of Saidai-ji's founding, the monk {{ill|Dōkyō|en|Dōkyō|WD=Q1272718|lt=Dōkyō}} held great influence in the political world, and it is believed that Dōkyō's ideological influence was also great in the construction of Saidai-ji Temple. The temple's name of "Saidai-ji" is a counterpoint to the great national temple of [[Tōdai-ji]] and thus a challenge to the existing political and religious power structure. The temple in its initial form was a huge complex and was counted as one of the {{ill|Nanto Shichi Daiji|en|Nanto Shichi Daiji|WD=Q1131210|lt=Seven Great Temples of Nara}}. It has two Main Halls: the Yakushi Kondō and the Miroku Kondō, numerous smaller halls (including the Shio-do, Juichimen-do), and twin five-story pagodas on the east and west. According to the "Zaizairyuki-cho", these halls housed many Buddhist statues and were decorated with many mirrors. In the Miroku Kondō alone, a total of 77 Buddhist statues were placed, and the Yakushi Kondō housed 21 statues. However, the temple fell quickly into decline during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, and many of its halls and pagodas were lost to fires and typhoons, and it came under the control of [[:en:Kofuku-ji|Kofuku-ji]]. The temple was restored in the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}} by the monk {{ill|Eison|en|Eison|WD=Q11410722|lt=Eison}}. He began the restoration in 1238 over a 50 years period worked to transform the temple into a center for social welfare work, especially to help the poor and sick. Many of the Buddhist statues and crafts that remain at the temple today were created during the time of Eison. The temple also produced many noteworthy priests, such as [[:en:Ninsho|Ninsho]], who worked to restore ruined temples of various provinces, including several of the ''{{ill|Provincial temple|en|Provincial temple|WD=Q1207757|lt=Provincial temple}}'' temples. In the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, the temple was burned down in December 1499 during the wars of the early {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}}, and its surviving East Pagoda was destroyed by a fire in 1502. During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the temple received a fief of 300 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku}}'' from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and began reconstruction. All of the current temple buildings were rebuilt after the Edo Period. Saidai-ji became independent from the [[Shingon]] sect in June 1895, and established the Shingon Ritsu sect. The temple houses numerous {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasures}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Properties}}. The precincts of Saidai-ji were designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1965. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2006|title=西大寺境内|trans-title= Saidai-ji keinai|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2022}} == Building list == *{{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall}} (Hondō – 本堂) – {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}}. It was rebuilt in 1808. *Shiō-dō (四王堂) – It was rebuilt in 1674. *Aizen-dō (愛染堂) – It was reconstructed in 1762. File:Saidai-ji Nara Japan03n.jpg|Shiō-dō File:Saidai-ji5.JPG|Aizen-dō File:Kishi Monju pentad (Saidaiji Nara).jpg|[[Manjushri|Manjushri Bodhisattva]] (Monju Bosatsu) ==See also== * {{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|WD=Q6591581|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)}} * {{ill|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|en|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|WD=Q3245518|lt=en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)}} * {{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)|WD=Q3245521|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)}} * [[:en:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] * {{ill|Nanto Shichi Daiji|en|Nanto Shichi Daiji|WD=Q1131210|lt=Nanto Shichi Daiji}}, Seven Great Temples of Nanto. * {{ill|Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato|en|Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato|WD=Q11433417|lt=Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato}} *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan [[arz:سيداى جى]][[co:commons:Category:Saidaiji (Nara)]][[cs:Saidaidži]][[de:Saidai-ji]][[en:Saidai-ji]][[es:Saidai-ji]][[fr:Saidai-ji]][[it:Saidai-ji]][[ja:西大寺 (奈良市)]][[ko:사이다이지]][[no:Saidai-ji]][[pt:Saidai-ji]][[zh:西大寺 (奈良市)]] [[arz:سيداى جى]][[co:commons:Category:Saidaiji (Nara)]][[cs:Saidaidži]][[de:Saidai-ji]][[en:Saidai-ji]][[es:Saidai-ji]][[fr:Saidai-ji]][[it:Saidai-ji]][[ja:西大寺 (奈良市)]][[ko:사이다이지]][[no:Saidai-ji]][[pt:Saidai-ji]][[zh:西大寺 (奈良市)]] [[arz:سيداى جى]][[sn:commons:Category:Saidaiji (Nara)]][[cs:Saidaidži]][[de:Saidai-ji]][[en:Saidai-ji]][[es:Saidai-ji]][[fr:Saidai-ji]][[it:Saidai-ji]][[ja:西大寺 (奈良市)]][[ko:사이다이지]][[no:Saidai-ji]][[pt:Saidai-ji]][[zh:西大寺 (奈良市)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Saidai-ji Nara Japan08bs3.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shingon Risshu|WD=Q580527}}|prefecture={{ill|Nara|WD=Q169134}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.69361634|N|135.77949822|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Jōtō|WD=Q11481733}}; {{ill|Kōken|WD=Q232955}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537}}|part_of={{ill|Nanto Shichi Daiji|WD=Q1131210}}; {{ill|Q11627440|WD=Q11627440}}; {{ill|Q22120745|WD=Q22120745}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}}; {{ill|Shingon sect eighteen Motoyama|WD=Q10437732}}; {{ill|Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato|WD=Q11433417}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1203935]] Wikidata name: Saidai-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Saidai-ji | native_name = 西大寺 | image = Saidai-ji Nara Japan08bs3.jpg | image_size = 300 | alt = | caption = [[:en:Hondō|Main Hall]] | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Saidai-ji | location = 1-1-5 Saidaiji Shibachō, Nara-shi, Nara-ken | coordinates = {{coord|34|41|37|N|135|46|46.2|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}} | rite = {{ill|Shingon Risshu|en|Shingon Risshu|WD=Q580527|lt=Shingon Ritsu}} | deity = [[:en:Shaka Nyorai|Shaka Nyorai]] (Shakyamuni Tathāgata)
({{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}}) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple | website = {{url|http://saidaiji.or.jp/}} | founded_by = [[Jōtō (monk)|Jōtō]] and {{ill|Empress Kōken|en|Empress Kōken|WD=Q232955|lt=Empress Kōken}} |established=765| year_completed = 1808 (Reconstruction) | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|41|37|N|135|46|46.2|E}}}}} }} {{nihongo|'''Saidai-ji'''|西大寺}} is a {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temple}} located in the Saidiaji-Shiba neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture}}, Japan. It became the head temple of the {{nihongo|{{ill|Shingon Risshu|en|Shingon Risshu|WD=Q580527|lt=Shingon Risshu}}|真言律宗}} sect after the sect's founder, {{nihongo|{{ill|Eison|en|Eison|WD=Q11410722|lt=Eison}}|叡尊}}, took over administration in 1238.{{cite book |last=Watt | first=Paul B. |editor-first=George| editor-last=Tanabe |title=Religions of Japan in Practice |publisher=Princeton University Press |date=March 8, 1999 |chapter=Chapter 7: Eison and the Shingon Vinaya Sect |isbn=0-691-05789-3}} The ''[[honzon]]'' of the temple is a statue of [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata|Shaka Nyorai]], enshrined by Eison in 1249. The temple was once one of the powerful {{ill|Nanto Shichi Daiji|en|Nanto Shichi Daiji|WD=Q1131210|lt=Seven Great Temples}} of the ancient capital of [[Heijō-kyō]]. ==History== According to the {{nihongo|''Saidaiji Zaizairyukicho''|西大寺資財流記帳|}} from 780, in September 764 when the retired [[:en:Empress Koken|Empress Koken]] requested the construction of a gilt bronze statue of the [[Four Heavenly Kings]] to pray for the end of the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|en|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|WD=Q11623017|lt=Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion}}. In October of the same year, Empress Koken re-enthroned as [[:en:Empress Shōtoku|Empress Shōtoku]]. The following year, in 765, the aforementioned statues of the Four Heavenly Kings were completed and Saidai-ji Temple was founded. These four statues are still enshrined in Saidai-ji's Shiōdō Hall, but only the demons that each statue steps on are from the time of the temple's founding, and the statues themselves have been replaced by later works. At the time of Saidai-ji's founding, the monk {{ill|Dōkyō|en|Dōkyō|WD=Q1272718|lt=Dōkyō}} held great influence in the political world, and it is believed that Dōkyō's ideological influence was also great in the construction of Saidai-ji Temple. The temple's name of "Saidai-ji" is a counterpoint to the great national temple of [[Tōdai-ji]] and thus a challenge to the existing political and religious power structure. The temple in its initial form was a huge complex and was counted as one of the {{ill|Nanto Shichi Daiji|en|Nanto Shichi Daiji|WD=Q1131210|lt=Seven Great Temples of Nara}}. It has two Main Halls: the Yakushi Kondō and the Miroku Kondō, numerous smaller halls (including the Shio-do, Juichimen-do), and twin five-story pagodas on the east and west. According to the "Zaizairyuki-cho", these halls housed many Buddhist statues and were decorated with many mirrors. In the Miroku Kondō alone, a total of 77 Buddhist statues were placed, and the Yakushi Kondō housed 21 statues. However, the temple fell quickly into decline during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, and many of its halls and pagodas were lost to fires and typhoons, and it came under the control of [[:en:Kofuku-ji|Kofuku-ji]]. The temple was restored in the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}} by the monk {{ill|Eison|en|Eison|WD=Q11410722|lt=Eison}}. He began the restoration in 1238 over a 50 years period worked to transform the temple into a center for social welfare work, especially to help the poor and sick. Many of the Buddhist statues and crafts that remain at the temple today were created during the time of Eison. The temple also produced many noteworthy priests, such as [[:en:Ninsho|Ninsho]], who worked to restore ruined temples of various provinces, including several of the ''{{ill|Provincial temple|en|Provincial temple|WD=Q1207757|lt=Provincial temple}}'' temples. In the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, the temple was burned down in December 1499 during the wars of the early {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}}, and its surviving East Pagoda was destroyed by a fire in 1502. During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the temple received a fief of 300 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku}}'' from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and began reconstruction. All of the current temple buildings were rebuilt after the Edo Period. Saidai-ji became independent from the [[Shingon]] sect in June 1895, and established the Shingon Ritsu sect. The temple houses numerous {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasures}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Properties}}. The precincts of Saidai-ji were designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1965. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2006|title=西大寺境内|trans-title= Saidai-ji keinai|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2022}} == Building list == *{{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall}} (Hondō – 本堂) – {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}}. It was rebuilt in 1808. *Shiō-dō (四王堂) – It was rebuilt in 1674. *Aizen-dō (愛染堂) – It was reconstructed in 1762. File:Saidai-ji Nara Japan03n.jpg|Shiō-dō File:Saidai-ji5.JPG|Aizen-dō File:Kishi Monju pentad (Saidaiji Nara).jpg|[[Manjushri|Manjushri Bodhisattva]] (Monju Bosatsu) ==See also== * {{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|WD=Q6591581|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)}} * {{ill|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|en|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|WD=Q3245518|lt=en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)}} * {{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)|WD=Q3245521|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)}} * [[:en:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] * {{ill|Nanto Shichi Daiji|en|Nanto Shichi Daiji|WD=Q1131210|lt=Nanto Shichi Daiji}}, Seven Great Temples of Nanto. * {{ill|Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato|en|Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato|WD=Q11433417|lt=Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato}} *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|WD=Q17050860|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * {{cite web|title=Saidai-ji Temple Homepage| language=Japanese| [[arz:سيداى جى]][[co:commons:Category:Saidaiji (Nara)]][[cs:Saidaidži]][[de:Saidai-ji]][[en:Saidai-ji]][[es:Saidai-ji]][[fr:Saidai-ji]][[it:Saidai-ji]][[ja:西大寺 (奈良市)]][[ko:사이다이지]][[no:Saidai-ji]][[pt:Saidai-ji]][[zh:西大寺 (奈良市)]] [[arz:سيداى جى]][[co:commons:Category:Saidaiji (Nara)]][[cs:Saidaidži]][[de:Saidai-ji]][[en:Saidai-ji]][[es:Saidai-ji]][[fr:Saidai-ji]][[it:Saidai-ji]][[ja:西大寺 (奈良市)]][[ko:사이다이지]][[no:Saidai-ji]][[pt:Saidai-ji]][[zh:西大寺 (奈良市)]] [[arz:سيداى جى]][[sn:commons:Category:Saidaiji (Nara)]][[cs:Saidaidži]][[de:Saidai-ji]][[en:Saidai-ji]][[es:Saidai-ji]][[fr:Saidai-ji]][[it:Saidai-ji]][[ja:西大寺 (奈良市)]][[ko:사이다이지]][[no:Saidai-ji]][[pt:Saidai-ji]][[zh:西大寺 (奈良市)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Saidai-ji Nara Japan08bs3.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shingon Risshu|WD=Q580527}}|prefecture={{ill|Nara|WD=Q169134}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.69361634|N|135.77949822|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Jōtō|WD=Q11481733}}; {{ill|Kōken|WD=Q232955}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537}}|part_of={{ill|Nanto Shichi Daiji|WD=Q1131210}}; {{ill|Q11627440|WD=Q11627440}}; {{ill|Q22120745|WD=Q22120745}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}}; {{ill|Shingon sect eighteen Motoyama|WD=Q10437732}}; {{ill|Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato|WD=Q11433417}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1203935]] Wikidata name: Saidai-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Saidai-ji | native_name = 西大寺 | image = Saidai-ji Nara Japan08bs3.jpg | image_size = 300 | alt = | caption = [[:en:Hondō|Main Hall]] | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Saidai-ji | location = 1-1-5 Saidaiji Shibachō, Nara-shi, Nara-ken | coordinates = {{coord|34|41|37|N|135|46|46.2|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}} | rite = {{ill|Shingon Risshu|en|Shingon Risshu|WD=Q580527|lt=Shingon Ritsu}} | deity = [[:en:Shaka Nyorai|Shaka Nyorai]] (Shakyamuni Tathāgata)
({{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}}) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple | website = {{url|http://saidaiji.or.jp/}} | founded_by = [[Jōtō (monk)|Jōtō]] and {{ill|Empress Kōken|en|Empress Kōken|WD=Q232955|lt=Empress Kōken}} |established=765| year_completed = 1808 (Reconstruction) | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|41|37|N|135|46|46.2|E}}}}} }} {{nihongo|'''Saidai-ji'''|西大寺}} is a {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temple}} located in the Saidiaji-Shiba neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture}}, Japan. It became the head temple of the {{nihongo|{{ill|Shingon Risshu|en|Shingon Risshu|WD=Q580527|lt=Shingon Risshu}}|真言律宗}} sect after the sect's founder, {{nihongo|{{ill|Eison|en|Eison|WD=Q11410722|lt=Eison}}|叡尊}}, took over administration in 1238.{{cite book |last=Watt | first=Paul B. |editor-first=George| editor-last=Tanabe |title=Religions of Japan in Practice |publisher=Princeton University Press |date=March 8, 1999 |chapter=Chapter 7: Eison and the Shingon Vinaya Sect |isbn=0-691-05789-3}} The ''[[honzon]]'' of the temple is a statue of [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata|Shaka Nyorai]], enshrined by Eison in 1249. The temple was once one of the powerful {{ill|Nanto Shichi Daiji|en|Nanto Shichi Daiji|WD=Q1131210|lt=Seven Great Temples}} of the ancient capital of [[Heijō-kyō]]. ==History== According to the {{nihongo|''Saidaiji Zaizairyukicho''|西大寺資財流記帳|}} from 780, in September 764 when the retired [[:en:Empress Koken|Empress Koken]] requested the construction of a gilt bronze statue of the [[Four Heavenly Kings]] to pray for the end of the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|en|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|WD=Q11623017|lt=Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion}}. In October of the same year, Empress Koken re-enthroned as [[:en:Empress Shōtoku|Empress Shōtoku]]. The following year, in 765, the aforementioned statues of the Four Heavenly Kings were completed and Saidai-ji Temple was founded. These four statues are still enshrined in Saidai-ji's Shiōdō Hall, but only the demons that each statue steps on are from the time of the temple's founding, and the statues themselves have been replaced by later works. At the time of Saidai-ji's founding, the monk {{ill|Dōkyō|en|Dōkyō|WD=Q1272718|lt=Dōkyō}} held great influence in the political world, and it is believed that Dōkyō's ideological influence was also great in the construction of Saidai-ji Temple. The temple's name of "Saidai-ji" is a counterpoint to the great national temple of [[Tōdai-ji]] and thus a challenge to the existing political and religious power structure. The temple in its initial form was a huge complex and was counted as one of the {{ill|Nanto Shichi Daiji|en|Nanto Shichi Daiji|WD=Q1131210|lt=Seven Great Temples of Nara}}. It has two Main Halls: the Yakushi Kondō and the Miroku Kondō, numerous smaller halls (including the Shio-do, Juichimen-do), and twin five-story pagodas on the east and west. According to the "Zaizairyuki-cho", these halls housed many Buddhist statues and were decorated with many mirrors. In the Miroku Kondō alone, a total of 77 Buddhist statues were placed, and the Yakushi Kondō housed 21 statues. However, the temple fell quickly into decline during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, and many of its halls and pagodas were lost to fires and typhoons, and it came under the control of [[:en:Kofuku-ji|Kofuku-ji]]. The temple was restored in the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}} by the monk {{ill|Eison|en|Eison|WD=Q11410722|lt=Eison}}. He began the restoration in 1238 over a 50 years period worked to transform the temple into a center for social welfare work, especially to help the poor and sick. Many of the Buddhist statues and crafts that remain at the temple today were created during the time of Eison. The temple also produced many noteworthy priests, such as [[:en:Ninsho|Ninsho]], who worked to restore ruined temples of various provinces, including several of the ''{{ill|Provincial temple|en|Provincial temple|WD=Q1207757|lt=Provincial temple}}'' temples. In the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, the temple was burned down in December 1499 during the wars of the early {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}}, and its surviving East Pagoda was destroyed by a fire in 1502. During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the temple received a fief of 300 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku}}'' from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and began reconstruction. All of the current temple buildings were rebuilt after the Edo Period. Saidai-ji became independent from the [[Shingon]] sect in June 1895, and established the Shingon Ritsu sect. The temple houses numerous {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasures}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Properties}}. The precincts of Saidai-ji were designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1965. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2006|title=西大寺境内|trans-title= Saidai-ji keinai|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2022}} == Building list == *{{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall}} (Hondō – 本堂) – {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}}. It was rebuilt in 1808. *Shiō-dō (四王堂) – It was rebuilt in 1674. *Aizen-dō (愛染堂) – It was reconstructed in 1762. File:Saidai-ji Nara Japan03n.jpg|Shiō-dō File:Saidai-ji5.JPG|Aizen-dō File:Kishi Monju pentad (Saidaiji Nara).jpg|[[Manjushri|Manjushri Bodhisattva]] (Monju Bosatsu) ==See also== * {{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|WD=Q6591581|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)}} * {{ill|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|en|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|WD=Q3245518|lt=en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)}} * {{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)|WD=Q3245521|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)}} * [[:en:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] * {{ill|Nanto Shichi Daiji|en|Nanto Shichi Daiji|WD=Q1131210|lt=Nanto Shichi Daiji}}, Seven Great Temples of Nanto. * {{ill|Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato|en|Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato|WD=Q11433417|lt=Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato}} *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|WD=Q17050860|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * {{cite web|title=Saidai-ji Temple Homepage| language=Japanese| url=http://www.naranet.co.jp/saidaiji/| accessdate = 2010-02-05}} {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category-inline|Saidaiji (Nara)|Saidai-ji}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} [[arz:سيداى جى]][[co:commons:Category:Saidaiji (Nara)]][[cs:Saidaidži]][[de:Saidai-ji]][[en:Saidai-ji]][[es:Saidai-ji]][[fr:Saidai-ji]][[it:Saidai-ji]][[ja:西大寺 (奈良市)]][[ko:사이다이지]][[no:Saidai-ji]][[pt:Saidai-ji]][[zh:西大寺 (奈良市)]] [[arz:سيداى جى]][[co:commons:Category:Saidaiji (Nara)]][[cs:Saidaidži]][[de:Saidai-ji]][[en:Saidai-ji]][[es:Saidai-ji]][[fr:Saidai-ji]][[it:Saidai-ji]][[ja:西大寺 (奈良市)]][[ko:사이다이지]][[no:Saidai-ji]][[pt:Saidai-ji]][[zh:西大寺 (奈良市)]] [[arz:سيداى جى]][[sn:commons:Category:Saidaiji (Nara)]][[cs:Saidaidži]][[de:Saidai-ji]][[en:Saidai-ji]][[es:Saidai-ji]][[fr:Saidai-ji]][[it:Saidai-ji]][[ja:西大寺 (奈良市)]][[ko:사이다이지]][[no:Saidai-ji]][[pt:Saidai-ji]][[zh:西大寺 (奈良市)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Saidai-ji Nara Japan08bs3.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shingon Risshu|WD=Q580527}}|prefecture={{ill|Nara|WD=Q169134}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.69361634|N|135.77949822|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Jōtō|WD=Q11481733}}; {{ill|Kōken|WD=Q232955}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537}}|part_of={{ill|Nanto Shichi Daiji|WD=Q1131210}}; {{ill|Q11627440|WD=Q11627440}}; {{ill|Q22120745|WD=Q22120745}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949}}; {{ill|Shingon sect eighteen Motoyama|WD=Q10437732}}; {{ill|Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato|WD=Q11433417}}}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Saidai-ji | native_name = 西大寺 | image = Saidai-ji Nara Japan08bs3.jpg | image_size = 300 | alt = | caption = [[:en:Hondō|Main Hall]] | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Saidai-ji | location = 1-1-5 Saidaiji Shibachō, Nara-shi, Nara-ken | coordinates = {{coord|34|41|37|N|135|46|46.2|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}} | rite = {{ill|Shingon Risshu|en|Shingon Risshu|WD=Q580527|lt=Shingon Ritsu}} | deity = [[:en:Shaka Nyorai|Shaka Nyorai]] (Shakyamuni Tathāgata)
({{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}}) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple | website = {{url|http://saidaiji.or.jp/}} | founded_by = [[Jōtō (monk)|Jōtō]] and {{ill|Empress Kōken|en|Empress Kōken|WD=Q232955|lt=Empress Kōken}} |established=765| year_completed = 1808 (Reconstruction) | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|41|37|N|135|46|46.2|E}}}}} }} {{nihongo|'''Saidai-ji'''|西大寺}} is a {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temple}} located in the Saidiaji-Shiba neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture}}, Japan. It became the head temple of the {{nihongo|{{ill|Shingon Risshu|en|Shingon Risshu|WD=Q580527|lt=Shingon Risshu}}|真言律宗}} sect after the sect's founder, {{nihongo|{{ill|Eison|en|Eison|WD=Q11410722|lt=Eison}}|叡尊}}, took over administration in 1238.{{cite book |last=Watt | first=Paul B. |editor-first=George| editor-last=Tanabe |title=Religions of Japan in Practice |publisher=Princeton University Press |date=March 8, 1999 |chapter=Chapter 7: Eison and the Shingon Vinaya Sect |isbn=0-691-05789-3}} The ''[[honzon]]'' of the temple is a statue of [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata|Shaka Nyorai]], enshrined by Eison in 1249. The temple was once one of the powerful {{ill|Nanto Shichi Daiji|en|Nanto Shichi Daiji|WD=Q1131210|lt=Seven Great Temples}} of the ancient capital of [[Heijō-kyō]]. ==History== According to the {{nihongo|''Saidaiji Zaizairyukicho''|西大寺資財流記帳|}} from 780, in September 764 when the retired [[:en:Empress Koken|Empress Koken]] requested the construction of a gilt bronze statue of the [[Four Heavenly Kings]] to pray for the end of the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|en|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|WD=Q11623017|lt=Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion}}. In October of the same year, Empress Koken re-enthroned as [[:en:Empress Shōtoku|Empress Shōtoku]]. The following year, in 765, the aforementioned statues of the Four Heavenly Kings were completed and Saidai-ji Temple was founded. These four statues are still enshrined in Saidai-ji's Shiōdō Hall, but only the demons that each statue steps on are from the time of the temple's founding, and the statues themselves have been replaced by later works. At the time of Saidai-ji's founding, the monk {{ill|Dōkyō|en|Dōkyō|WD=Q1272718|lt=Dōkyō}} held great influence in the political world, and it is believed that Dōkyō's ideological influence was also great in the construction of Saidai-ji Temple. The temple's name of "Saidai-ji" is a counterpoint to the great national temple of [[Tōdai-ji]] and thus a challenge to the existing political and religious power structure. The temple in its initial form was a huge complex and was counted as one of the {{ill|Nanto Shichi Daiji|en|Nanto Shichi Daiji|WD=Q1131210|lt=Seven Great Temples of Nara}}. It has two Main Halls: the Yakushi Kondō and the Miroku Kondō, numerous smaller halls (including the Shio-do, Juichimen-do), and twin five-story pagodas on the east and west. According to the "Zaizairyuki-cho", these halls housed many Buddhist statues and were decorated with many mirrors. In the Miroku Kondō alone, a total of 77 Buddhist statues were placed, and the Yakushi Kondō housed 21 statues. However, the temple fell quickly into decline during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, and many of its halls and pagodas were lost to fires and typhoons, and it came under the control of [[:en:Kofuku-ji|Kofuku-ji]]. The temple was restored in the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}} by the monk {{ill|Eison|en|Eison|WD=Q11410722|lt=Eison}}. He began the restoration in 1238 over a 50 years period worked to transform the temple into a center for social welfare work, especially to help the poor and sick. Many of the Buddhist statues and crafts that remain at the temple today were created during the time of Eison. The temple also produced many noteworthy priests, such as [[:en:Ninsho|Ninsho]], who worked to restore ruined temples of various provinces, including several of the ''{{ill|Provincial temple|en|Provincial temple|WD=Q1207757|lt=Provincial temple}}'' temples. In the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, the temple was burned down in December 1499 during the wars of the early {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}}, and its surviving East Pagoda was destroyed by a fire in 1502. During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the temple received a fief of 300 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku}}'' from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and began reconstruction. All of the current temple buildings were rebuilt after the Edo Period. Saidai-ji became independent from the [[Shingon]] sect in June 1895, and established the Shingon Ritsu sect. The temple houses numerous {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasures}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Properties}}. The precincts of Saidai-ji were designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1965. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2006|title=西大寺境内|trans-title= Saidai-ji keinai|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2022}} == Building list == *{{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall}} (Hondō – 本堂) – {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property}}. It was rebuilt in 1808. *Shiō-dō (四王堂) – It was rebuilt in 1674. *Aizen-dō (愛染堂) – It was reconstructed in 1762. File:Saidai-ji Nara Japan03n.jpg|Shiō-dō File:Saidai-ji5.JPG|Aizen-dō File:Kishi Monju pentad (Saidaiji Nara).jpg|[[Manjushri|Manjushri Bodhisattva]] (Monju Bosatsu) ==See also== * {{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|WD=Q6591581|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)}} * {{ill|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|en|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|WD=Q3245518|lt=en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)}} * {{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)|WD=Q3245521|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)}} * [[:en:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] * {{ill|Nanto Shichi Daiji|en|Nanto Shichi Daiji|WD=Q1131210|lt=Nanto Shichi Daiji}}, Seven Great Temples of Nanto. * {{ill|Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato|en|Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato|WD=Q11433417|lt=Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato}} *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|WD=Q17050860|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * {{cite web|title=Saidai-ji Temple Homepage| language=Japanese| url=http://www.naranet.co.jp/saidaiji/| accessdate = 2010-02-05}} {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category-inline|Saidaiji (Nara)|Saidai-ji}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{ill|Kōken|WD=Q232955|1=Empress Kōken}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata}}|part_of={{ill|Nanto Shichi Daiji|WD=Q1131210|1=Nanto Shichi Daiji}}; {{ill|Q11627440|WD=Q11627440|1=Q11627440}}; {{ill|Q22120745|WD=Q22120745|1=Yamato Northern 88 Sacred Sites}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949|1=Sacred Sites of Kamigata}}; {{ill|Shingon sect eighteen Motoyama|WD=Q10437732|1=Shingon sect eighteen Motoyama}}; {{ill|Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato|WD=Q11433417|1=Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato}}}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Saidai-ji | native_name = 西大寺 | image = Saidai-ji Nara Japan08bs3.jpg | image_size = 300 | alt = | caption = [[:en:Hondō|Main Hall]] | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Saidai-ji | location = 1-1-5 Saidaiji Shibachō, Nara-shi, Nara-ken | coordinates = {{coord|34|41|37|N|135|46|46.2|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}} | rite = {{ill|Shingon Risshu|en|Shingon Risshu|WD=Q580527|lt=Shingon Ritsu|1=Shingon Risshu}} | deity = [[:en:Shaka Nyorai|Shaka Nyorai]] (Shakyamuni Tathāgata)
({{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple | website = {{url|http://saidaiji.or.jp/}} | founded_by = [[Jōtō (monk)|Jōtō]] and {{ill|Empress Kōken|en|Empress Kōken|WD=Q232955|lt=Empress Kōken|1=Empress Kōken}} |established=765| year_completed = 1808 (Reconstruction) | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|41|37|N|135|46|46.2|E}}}}} }} {{nihongo|'''Saidai-ji'''|西大寺}} is a {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temple|1=Buddhist temples in Japan}} located in the Saidiaji-Shiba neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}, Japan. It became the head temple of the {{nihongo|{{ill|Shingon Risshu|en|Shingon Risshu|WD=Q580527|lt=Shingon Risshu|1=Shingon Risshu}}|真言律宗}} sect after the sect's founder, {{nihongo|{{ill|Eison|en|Eison|WD=Q11410722|lt=Eison|1=Eison}}|叡尊}}, took over administration in 1238.{{cite book |last=Watt | first=Paul B. |editor-first=George| editor-last=Tanabe |title=Religions of Japan in Practice |publisher=Princeton University Press |date=March 8, 1999 |chapter=Chapter 7: Eison and the Shingon Vinaya Sect |isbn=0-691-05789-3}} The ''[[honzon]]'' of the temple is a statue of [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata|Shaka Nyorai]], enshrined by Eison in 1249. The temple was once one of the powerful {{ill|Nanto Shichi Daiji|en|Nanto Shichi Daiji|WD=Q1131210|lt=Seven Great Temples|1=Nanto Shichi Daiji}} of the ancient capital of [[Heijō-kyō]]. ==History== According to the {{nihongo|''Saidaiji Zaizairyukicho''|西大寺資財流記帳|}} from 780, in September 764 when the retired [[:en:Empress Koken|Empress Koken]] requested the construction of a gilt bronze statue of the [[Four Heavenly Kings]] to pray for the end of the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|en|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|WD=Q11623017|lt=Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|1=Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion}}. In October of the same year, Empress Koken re-enthroned as [[:en:Empress Shōtoku|Empress Shōtoku]]. The following year, in 765, the aforementioned statues of the Four Heavenly Kings were completed and Saidai-ji Temple was founded. These four statues are still enshrined in Saidai-ji's Shiōdō Hall, but only the demons that each statue steps on are from the time of the temple's founding, and the statues themselves have been replaced by later works. At the time of Saidai-ji's founding, the monk {{ill|Dōkyō|en|Dōkyō|WD=Q1272718|lt=Dōkyō|1=Dōkyō}} held great influence in the political world, and it is believed that Dōkyō's ideological influence was also great in the construction of Saidai-ji Temple. The temple's name of "Saidai-ji" is a counterpoint to the great national temple of [[Tōdai-ji]] and thus a challenge to the existing political and religious power structure. The temple in its initial form was a huge complex and was counted as one of the {{ill|Nanto Shichi Daiji|en|Nanto Shichi Daiji|WD=Q1131210|lt=Seven Great Temples of Nara|1=Nanto Shichi Daiji}}. It has two Main Halls: the Yakushi Kondō and the Miroku Kondō, numerous smaller halls (including the Shio-do, Juichimen-do), and twin five-story pagodas on the east and west. According to the "Zaizairyuki-cho", these halls housed many Buddhist statues and were decorated with many mirrors. In the Miroku Kondō alone, a total of 77 Buddhist statues were placed, and the Yakushi Kondō housed 21 statues. However, the temple fell quickly into decline during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, and many of its halls and pagodas were lost to fires and typhoons, and it came under the control of [[:en:Kofuku-ji|Kofuku-ji]]. The temple was restored in the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} by the monk {{ill|Eison|en|Eison|WD=Q11410722|lt=Eison|1=Eison}}. He began the restoration in 1238 over a 50 years period worked to transform the temple into a center for social welfare work, especially to help the poor and sick. Many of the Buddhist statues and crafts that remain at the temple today were created during the time of Eison. The temple also produced many noteworthy priests, such as [[:en:Ninsho|Ninsho]], who worked to restore ruined temples of various provinces, including several of the ''{{ill|Provincial temple|en|Provincial temple|WD=Q1207757|lt=Provincial temple|1=Provincial temple}}'' temples. In the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, the temple was burned down in December 1499 during the wars of the early {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}, and its surviving East Pagoda was destroyed by a fire in 1502. During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the temple received a fief of 300 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku|1=Koku}}'' from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and began reconstruction. All of the current temple buildings were rebuilt after the Edo Period. Saidai-ji became independent from the [[Shingon]] sect in June 1895, and established the Shingon Ritsu sect. The temple houses numerous {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasures|1=National Treasure (Japan)}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Properties|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}. The precincts of Saidai-ji were designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1965. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2006|title=西大寺境内|trans-title= Saidai-ji keinai|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2022}} == Building list == *{{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall|1=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)}} (Hondō – 本堂) – {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}. It was rebuilt in 1808. *Shiō-dō (四王堂) – It was rebuilt in 1674. *Aizen-dō (愛染堂) – It was reconstructed in 1762. File:Saidai-ji Nara Japan03n.jpg|Shiō-dō File:Saidai-ji5.JPG|Aizen-dō File:Kishi Monju pentad (Saidaiji Nara).jpg|[[Manjushri|Manjushri Bodhisattva]] (Monju Bosatsu) ==See also== * {{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|WD=Q6591581|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)}} * {{ill|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|en|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|WD=Q3245518|lt=en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)}} * {{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)|WD=Q3245521|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)}} * [[:en:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] * {{ill|Nanto Shichi Daiji|en|Nanto Shichi Daiji|WD=Q1131210|lt=Nanto Shichi Daiji|1=Nanto Shichi Daiji}}, Seven Great Temples of Nanto. * {{ill|Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato|en|Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato|WD=Q11433417|lt=Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato|1=Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato}} *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|WD=Q17050860|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * {{cite web|title=Saidai-ji Temple Homepage| language=Japanese| url=http://www.naranet.co.jp/saidaiji/| accessdate = 2010-02-05}} {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category-inline|Saidaiji However, the temple fell quickly into decline during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, and many of its halls and pagodas were lost to fires and typhoons, and it came under the control of [[:en:Kofuku-ji|Kofuku-ji]]. The temple was restored in the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} by the monk {{ill|Eison|en|Eison|WD=Q11410722|lt=Eison|1=Eison}}. The precincts of Saidai-ji were designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1965. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2006|title=西大寺境内|trans-title= Saidai-ji keinai|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2022}} == Building list == *{{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall|1=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)}} (Hondō – 本堂) – {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}. [[arz:سيداى جى]][[co:commons:Category:Saidaiji (Nara)]][[cs:Saidaidži]][[de:Saidai-ji]][[en:Saidai-ji]][[es:Saidai-ji]][[fr:Saidai-ji]][[it:Saidai-ji]][[ja:西大寺 (奈良市)]][[ko:사이다이지]][[no:Saidai-ji]][[pt:Saidai-ji]][[zh:西大寺 (奈良市)]] [[arz:سيداى جى]][[co:commons:Category:Saidaiji (Nara)]][[cs:Saidaidži]][[de:Saidai-ji]][[en:Saidai-ji]][[es:Saidai-ji]][[fr:Saidai-ji]][[it:Saidai-ji]][[ja:西大寺 (奈良市)]][[ko:사이다이지]][[no:Saidai-ji]][[pt:Saidai-ji]][[zh:西大寺 (奈良市)]] [[arz:سيداى جى]][[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Saidaiji (Nara)]][[cs:Saidaidži]][[de:Saidai-ji]][[en:Saidai-ji]][[es:Saidai-ji]][[fr:Saidai-ji]][[it:Saidai-ji]][[ja:西大寺 (奈良市)]][[ko:사이다이지]][[no:Saidai-ji]][[pt:Saidai-ji]][[zh:西大寺 (奈良市)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Saidai-ji Nara Japan08bs3.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shingon Risshu|WD=Q580527|1=Shingon Risshu}}|prefecture={{ill|Nara|WD=Q169134|1=Nara (city)}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.69361634|N|135.77949822|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Jōtō|WD=Q11481733|1=Jōtō}}; {{ill|Kōken|WD=Q232955|1=Empress Kōken}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata}}|part_of={{ill|Nanto Shichi Daiji|WD=Q1131210|1=Nanto Shichi Daiji}}; {{ill|Q11627440|WD=Q11627440|1=Q11627440}}; {{ill|Q22120745|WD=Q22120745|1=Yamato Northern 88 Sacred Sites}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949|1=Sacred Sites of Kamigata}}; {{ill|Shingon sect eighteen Motoyama|WD=Q10437732|1=Shingon sect eighteen Motoyama}}; {{ill|Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato|WD=Q11433417|1=Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato}}}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Saidai-ji | native_name = 西大寺 | image = Saidai-ji Nara Japan08bs3.jpg | image_size = 300 | alt = | caption = [[:en:Hondō|Main Hall]] | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Saidai-ji | location = 1-1-5 Saidaiji Shibachō, Nara-shi, Nara-ken | coordinates = {{coord|34|41|37|N|135|46|46.2|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}} | rite = {{ill|Shingon Risshu|en|Shingon Risshu|WD=Q580527|lt=Shingon Ritsu|1=Shingon Risshu}} | deity = [[:en:Shaka Nyorai|Shaka Nyorai]] (Shakyamuni Tathāgata)
({{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple | website = {{url|http://saidaiji.or.jp/}} | founded_by = [[Jōtō (monk)|Jōtō]] and {{ill|Empress Kōken|en|Empress Kōken|WD=Q232955|lt=Empress Kōken|1=Empress Kōken}} |established=765| year_completed = 1808 (Reconstruction) | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|41|37|N|135|46|46.2|E}}}}} }} {{nihongo|'''Saidai-ji'''|西大寺}} is a {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temple|1=Buddhist temples in Japan}} located in the Saidiaji-Shiba neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}, Japan. It became the head temple of the {{nihongo|{{ill|Shingon Risshu|en|Shingon Risshu|WD=Q580527|lt=Shingon Risshu|1=Shingon Risshu}}|真言律宗}} sect after the sect's founder, {{nihongo|{{ill|Eison|en|Eison|WD=Q11410722|lt=Eison|1=Eison}}|叡尊}}, took over administration in 1238.{{cite book |last=Watt | first=Paul B. |editor-first=George| editor-last=Tanabe |title=Religions of Japan in Practice |publisher=Princeton University Press |date=March 8, 1999 |chapter=Chapter 7: Eison and the Shingon Vinaya Sect |isbn=0-691-05789-3}} The ''[[honzon]]'' of the temple is a statue of [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata|Shaka Nyorai]], enshrined by Eison in 1249. The temple was once one of the powerful {{ill|Nanto Shichi Daiji|en|Nanto Shichi Daiji|WD=Q1131210|lt=Seven Great Temples|1=Nanto Shichi Daiji}} of the ancient capital of [[Heijō-kyō]]. ==History== According to the {{nihongo|''Saidaiji Zaizairyukicho''|西大寺資財流記帳|}} from 780, in September 764 when the retired [[:en:Empress Koken|Empress Koken]] requested the construction of a gilt bronze statue of the [[Four Heavenly Kings]] to pray for the end of the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|en|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|WD=Q11623017|lt=Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|1=Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion}}. In October of the same year, Empress Koken re-enthroned as [[:en:Empress Shōtoku|Empress Shōtoku]]. The following year, in 765, the aforementioned statues of the Four Heavenly Kings were completed and Saidai-ji Temple was founded. These four statues are still enshrined in Saidai-ji's Shiōdō Hall, but only the demons that each statue steps on are from the time of the temple's founding, and the statues themselves have been replaced by later works. At the time of Saidai-ji's founding, the monk {{ill|Dōkyō|en|Dōkyō|WD=Q1272718|lt=Dōkyō|1=Dōkyō}} held great influence in the political world, and it is believed that Dōkyō's ideological influence was also great in the construction of Saidai-ji Temple. The temple's name of "Saidai-ji" is a counterpoint to the great national temple of [[Tōdai-ji]] and thus a challenge to the existing political and religious power structure. The temple in its initial form was a huge complex and was counted as one of the {{ill|Nanto Shichi Daiji|en|Nanto Shichi Daiji|WD=Q1131210|lt=Seven Great Temples of Nara|1=Nanto Shichi Daiji}}. It has two Main Halls: the Yakushi Kondō and the Miroku Kondō, numerous smaller halls (including the Shio-do, Juichimen-do), and twin five-story pagodas on the east and west. According to the "Zaizairyuki-cho", these halls housed many Buddhist statues and were decorated with many mirrors. In the Miroku Kondō alone, a total of 77 Buddhist statues were placed, and the Yakushi Kondō housed 21 statues. However, the temple fell quickly into decline during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, and many of its halls and pagodas were lost to fires and typhoons, and it came under the control of [[:en:Kofuku-ji|Kofuku-ji]]. The temple was restored in the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} by the monk {{ill|Eison|en|Eison|WD=Q11410722|lt=Eison|1=Eison}}. He began the restoration in 1238 over a 50 years period worked to transform the temple into a center for social welfare work, especially to help the poor and sick. Many of the Buddhist statues and crafts that remain at the temple today were created during the time of Eison. The temple also produced many noteworthy priests, such as [[:en:Ninsho|Ninsho]], who worked to restore ruined temples of various provinces, including several of the ''{{ill|Provincial temple|en|Provincial temple|WD=Q1207757|lt=Provincial temple|1=Provincial temple}}'' temples. In the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, the temple was burned down in December 1499 during the wars of the early {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}, and its surviving East Pagoda was destroyed by a fire in 1502. During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the temple received a fief of 300 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku|1=Koku}}'' from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and began reconstruction. All of the current temple buildings were rebuilt after the Edo Period. Saidai-ji became independent from the [[Shingon]] sect in June 1895, and established the Shingon Ritsu sect. The temple houses numerous {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasures|1=National Treasure (Japan)}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Properties|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}. The precincts of Saidai-ji were designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1965. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2006|title=西大寺境内|trans-title= Saidai-ji keinai|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2022}} == Building list == *{{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall|1=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)}} (Hondō – 本堂) – {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}. It was rebuilt in 1808. *Shiō-dō (四王堂) – It was rebuilt in 1674. *Aizen-dō (愛染堂) – It was reconstructed in 1762. File:Saidai-ji Nara Japan03n.jpg|Shiō-dō File:Saidai-ji5.JPG|Aizen-dō File:Kishi Monju pentad (Saidaiji Nara).jpg|[[Manjushri|Manjushri Bodhisattva]] (Monju Bosatsu) ==See also== * {{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|WD=Q6591581|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)}} * {{ill|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|en|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|WD=Q3245518|lt=en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)}} * {{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)|WD=Q3245521|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)}} * [[:en:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] * {{ill|Nanto Shichi Daiji|en|Nanto Shichi Daiji|WD=Q1131210|lt=Nanto Shichi Daiji|1=Nanto Shichi Daiji}}, Seven Great Temples of Nanto. * {{ill|Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato|en|Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato|WD=Q11433417|lt=Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato|1=Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato}} *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|WD=Q17050860|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== * {{cite web|title=Saidai-ji Temple Homepage| language=Japanese| url=http://www.naranet.co.jp/saidaiji/| accessdate = 2010-02-05}} {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category-inline|Saidaiji (Nara)|Saidai-ji}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} [[file:200730 Model of Saidaiji in Heijokyo viewed from north.jpg|thumb|right|280px|Model of Nara period Saidai-ji]] [[en:Saidai-ji]] [[arz:سيداى جى]] It was rebuilt in 1808. *Shiō-dō (四王堂) – It was rebuilt in 1674. *Aizen-dō (愛染堂) – It was reconstructed in 1762. [[arz:سيداى جى]][[co:commons:Category:Saidaiji (Nara)]][[cs:Saidaidži]][[de:Saidai-ji]][[en:Saidai-ji]][[es:Saidai-ji]][[fr:Saidai-ji]][[it:Saidai-ji]][[ja:西大寺 (奈良市)]][[ko:사이다이지]][[no:Saidai-ji]][[pt:Saidai-ji]][[zh:西大寺 (奈良市)]] [[arz:سيداى جى]][[co:commons:Category:Saidaiji (Nara)]][[cs:Saidaidži]][[de:Saidai-ji]][[en:Saidai-ji]][[es:Saidai-ji]][[fr:Saidai-ji]][[it:Saidai-ji]][[ja:西大寺 (奈良市)]][[ko:사이다이지]][[no:Saidai-ji]][[pt:Saidai-ji]][[zh:西大寺 (奈良市)]] [[arz:سيداى جى]][[cs:Saidaidži]][[de:Saidai-ji]][[en:Saidai-ji]][[es:Saidai-ji]][[fr:Saidai-ji]][[it:Saidai-ji]][[ja:西大寺 (奈良市)]][[ko:사이다이지]][[no:Saidai-ji]][[pt:Saidai-ji]][[zh:西大寺 (奈良市)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Saidai-ji Nara Japan08bs3.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shingon Risshu|WD=Q580527|1=Shingon Risshu}}|prefecture={{ill|Nara|WD=Q169134|1=Nara (city)}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.69361634|N|135.77949822|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Jōtō|WD=Q11481733|1=Jōtō}}; {{ill|Kōken|WD=Q232955|1=Empress Kōken}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata}}|part_of={{ill|Nanto Shichi Daiji|WD=Q1131210|1=Nanto Shichi Daiji}}; {{ill|Q11627440|WD=Q11627440|1=Q11627440}}; {{ill|Q22120745|WD=Q22120745|1=Yamato Northern 88 Sacred Sites}}; {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|WD=Q10444949|1=Sacred Sites of Kamigata}}; {{ill|Shingon sect eighteen Motoyama|WD=Q10437732|1=Shingon sect eighteen Motoyama}}; {{ill|Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato|WD=Q11433417|1=Thirteen Buddhist Sites of Yamato}}}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Saidai-ji | native_name = 西大寺 | image = Saidai-ji Nara Japan08bs3.jpg | image_size = 300 | alt = | caption = [[:en:Hondō|Main Hall]] | map_type = Japan Nara Prefecture#Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Saidai-ji | location = 1-1-5 Saidaiji Shibachō, Nara-shi, Nara-ken | coordinates = {{coord|34|41|37|N|135|46|46.2|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}} | rite = {{ill|Shingon Risshu|en|Shingon Risshu|WD=Q580527|lt=Shingon Ritsu|1=Shingon Risshu}} | deity = [[:en:Shaka Nyorai|Shaka Nyorai]] (Shakyamuni Tathāgata)
({{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple | website = {{url|http://saidaiji.or.jp/}} | founded_by = [[Jōtō (monk)|Jōtō]] and {{ill|Empress Kōken|en|Empress Kōken|WD=Q232955|lt=Empress Kōken|1=Empress Kōken}} |established=765| year_completed = 1808 (Reconstruction) | footnotes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|41|37|N|135|46|46.2|E}}}}} }} {{nihongo|'''Saidai-ji'''|西大寺}} is a {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temple|1=Buddhist temples in Japan}} located in the Saidiaji-Shiba neighborhood of the city of {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}, Japan. It became the head temple of the {{nihongo|{{ill|Shingon Risshu|en|Shingon Risshu|WD=Q580527|lt=Shingon Risshu|1=Shingon Risshu}}|真言律宗}} sect after the sect's founder, {{nihongo|{{ill|Eison|en|Eison|WD=Q11410722|lt=Eison|1=Eison}}|叡尊}}, took over administration in 1238.{{cite book |last=Watt | first=Paul B. |editor-first=George| editor-last=Tanabe |title=Religions of Japan in Practice |publisher=Princeton University Press |date=March 8, 1999 |chapter=Chapter 7: Eison and the Shingon Vinaya Sect |isbn=0-691-05789-3}} The ''[[honzon]]'' of the temple is a statue of [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata|Shaka Nyorai]], enshrined by Eison in 1249. The temple was once one of the powerful {{ill|Nanto Shichi Daiji|en|Nanto Shichi Daiji|WD=Q1131210|lt=Seven Great Temples|1=Nanto Shichi Daiji}} of the ancient capital of [[Heijō-kyō]]. ==History== According to the {{nihongo|''Saidaiji Zaizairyukicho''|西大寺資財流記帳|}} from 780, in September 764 when the retired [[:en:Empress Koken|Empress Koken]] requested the construction of a gilt bronze statue of the [[Four Heavenly Kings]] to pray for the end of the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|en|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|WD=Q11623017|lt=Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|1=Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion}}. In October of the same year, Empress Koken re-enthroned as [[:en:Empress Shōtoku|Empress Shōtoku]]. The following year, in 765, the aforementioned statues of the Four Heavenly Kings were completed and Saidai-ji Temple was founded. These four statues are still enshrined in Saidai-ji's Shiōdō Hall, but only the demons that each statue steps on are from the time of the temple's founding, and the statues themselves have been replaced by later works. At the time of Saidai-ji's founding, the monk {{ill|Dōkyō|en|Dōkyō|WD=Q1272718|lt=Dōkyō|1=Dōkyō}} held great influence in the political world, and it is believed that Dōkyō's ideological influence was also great in the construction of Saidai-ji Temple. The temple's name of "Saidai-ji" is a counterpoint to the great national temple of [[Tōdai-ji]] and thus a challenge to the existing political and religious power structure. The temple in its initial form was a huge complex and was counted as one of the {{ill|Nanto Shichi Daiji|en|Nanto Shichi Daiji|WD=Q1131210|lt=Seven Great Temples of Nara|1=Nanto Shichi Daiji}}. It has two Main Halls: the Yakushi Kondō and the Miroku Kondō, numerous smaller halls (including the Shio-do, Juichimen-do), and twin five-story pagodas on the east and west. According to the "Zaizairyuki-cho", these halls housed many Buddhist statues and were decorated with many mirrors. In the Miroku Kondō alone, a total of 77 Buddhist statues were placed, and the Yakushi Kondō housed 21 statues. However, the temple fell quickly into decline during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, and many of its halls and pagodas were lost to fires and typhoons, and it came under the control of [[:en:Kofuku-ji|Kofuku-ji]]. The temple was restored in the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} by the monk {{ill|Eison|en|Eison|WD=Q11410722|lt=Eison|1=Eison}}. He began the restoration in 1238 over a 50 years period worked to transform the temple into a center for social welfare work, especially to help the poor and sick. Many of the Buddhist statues and crafts that remain at the temple today were created during the time of Eison. The temple also produced many noteworthy priests, such as [[:en:Ninsho|Ninsho]], who worked to restore ruined temples of various provinces, including several of the ''{{ill|Provincial temple|en|Provincial temple|WD=Q1207757|lt=Provincial temple|1=Provincial temple}}'' temples. In the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, the temple was burned down in December 1499 during the wars of the early {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}, and its surviving East Pagoda was destroyed by a fire in 1502. During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the temple received a fief of 300 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku|1=Koku}}'' from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and began reconstruction. All of the current temple buildings were rebuilt after the Edo Period. Saidai-ji became independent from the [[Shingon]] sect in June 1895, and established the Shingon Ritsu sect. The temple houses numerous {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasures|1=National Treasure (Japan)}} and {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Properties|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}. The precincts of Saidai-ji were designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1965. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/2006|title=西大寺境内|trans-title= Saidai-ji keinai|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2022}} == Building list == *{{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall|1=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)}} (Hondō – 本堂) – {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}. It was rebuilt in 1808. *Shiō-dō (四王堂) – It was rebuilt in 1674. *Aizen-dō (愛染堂) – It was reconstructed in 1762. File:Saidai-ji Nara Japan03n.jpg|Shiō-dō File:Saidai-ji5.JPG|Aizen-dō File:Kishi Monju pentad (Saidaiji Nara).jpg|[[Manjushri|Manjushri Bodhisattva]] (Monju Bosatsu) ==See also== * {{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|WD=Q6591581|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (crafts: others)}} * {{ill|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|en|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|WD=Q3245518|lt=en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)}} * {{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (sculptures)|WD=Q3245521|lt=List of
